responseSet.title: Structural Change In Psychoanalysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Structural Change In Psychoanalysis

Edward Weinshel M.D.

Edward Weinshel

Edward M. Weinshel, M.D.
2625 Scott Street
San Francisco, CA 94123

Edward Weinshel  

This paper demonstrates three points: First, the disadvantages and problems related to the concept of "structural change," especially in the course of clinical psychoanalysis, and the advantages in replacing that term with "psychoanalytic change." Second, the ways in which following the progress of analytic work "in terms of the vicissitudes of the resistances as they emerge, are recognized, interpreted, and analyzed" are the most effective method of describing the psychoanalytic process and "psychoanalytic change." Third, four sessions in the second year of the analysis of a male patient (together with some background material) are offered to demonstrate how these theoretical concepts and technical principles are utilized in the psychoanalytic work.

responseSet.title: The Concept Of Adult Psychic Structure
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept Of Adult Psychic Structure

Jon Meyer M.D.

Jon Meyer

Jon K. Meyer, M.D.
2321 East Stratford Court
Milwaukee, WI 53211

Jon Meyer  

This paper briefly reviews a variety of perspectives on the structural construct. Included in the review are elements of Freud's thought regarding structure both prior to and after The Ego and the Id. Also included are the ideas of Hartmann, Rapaport, Arlow, Brenner, Gill, and others who have made contributions leading to the preeminence of the structural model. Unfortunately, precisely what is meant by structure is not always clear. Some of the controversies surrounding the meaning of structural concepts seem related to two different usages of the term "structure": one referring to structure in the broad context as a stable organization of various mental elements, and the other referring specifically to the formulations of the tripartite model. From one point of view, the tripartite model constitutes a narrow paradigm within psychoanalysis, specifically applicable to the clinical situation, while the broader concept constitutes a more general scientific paradigm interfacing with similar concepts in ther disciplines.

responseSet.title: "The Talking Cure": A Descriptive Guide to Psychoanalysis: By Joseph D. Lichtenberg, M.D. Hillsdale, N.J.:  Analytic Press, 1985, 152 pp., $19.95.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

"The Talking Cure": A Descriptive Guide to Psychoanalysis

By Joseph D. Lichtenberg, M.D. Hillsdale, N.J.: Analytic Press, 1985, 152 pp., $19.95.

George Pollock M.D.

George Pollock

George H. Pollock, M.D.
Chicago, IL

George Pollock  

Lichtenberg has produced a guide for those who are interested in learning more about psychoanalytic treatment and providing answers to questions a prospective patient may ask. The volume is interspersed with clinical illustrations to exemplify points the author wishes to emphasize. These case vignettes provide a "taste" of what psychoanalysts do and what they understand about their patients.

Lichtenberg nicely divides his book into sections that illustrate the process of recognizing the need for help, finding a qualified psychoanalyst with whom one may wish to work, the initial consultation, the beginning of the psychoanalytic treatment—including the agreement to the analytic "contract," the use of the couch, and of free association—the various phases of the psychoanalytic treatment, and finally, the termination phase.

The writing is clear and uncomplicated by technical language. Various concepts such as psychic determinism and transference are simply and briefly described. Lichtenberg addresses questions that are often asked but rarely written about, e.g., the influence of the analyst's personality, race, ethnic background, age, sex, or religion on the analytic relationship. He addresses the kinds of questions an analyst asks in the consultation, how the analyst arrives at a diagnostic impression and a subsequent recommendation to the potential patient.

The arrangements for the beginning of analytic treatment include such practical "contractual" items as setting the fee, appointment times, continuity of therapy, missed appointments, vacations, holidays, as well as more technical considerations, e.g., making no major decisions while in psychoanalytic treatment, use of the couch, and free association. The actual beginning or opening phase is again discussed by Lichtenberg in a simple, direct, and clear fashion. The understanding of how one's mind works, defenses, themes and patterns that are recurrent, and how these relate to the analyst and the analytic situation follow and lead into a discussion of the middle phase of the analytic treatment. In this chapter there is a brief discussion of psychic conflict, regression, resistance, progression, and the working-through process. The final chapter deals with termination, how it is arranged, the setting of the ending date, and the "revisiting of old problems." In addition to the clear clinical exemplars, Lichtenberg adds his own commentaries and observations throughout.

[[RunningHead]]

Infants and Parents: Clinical Case Reports, No. 2

Edited by Sally Provence. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1983, x + 306 pp., $32.50.

Alice Frankel M.D.

Alice Frankel

Alice Kross Frankel, M.D.
Larchmont, NY

Alice Frankel  

Infants and Parents is a publication of the National Center for Clinical Infant Programs. Since its establishment in 1977 the Center has been an effective resource and disseminator of information in the burgeoning field of infant psychiatry. Among its many valuable contributions is its series, Clinical Infant Reports.

Sally Provence, one of the founders of the National Center for Clinical Infant Programs, was a natural choice as editor. In her excellent introduction she lays out the book's purpose, an effort to "… share experience … telling the stories of individuals who touch, puzzle, challenge and inevitably instruct us …" She describes the "shared assumptions" that inform all the reports despite their clinical heterogeneity.

Child Maltreatment: A Handbook for Mental Health and Child Care Professionals

By Arthur H. Green, M.D. New York: Aronson, 1980, xii + 314 pp., $25.00.

Melvin Scharfman M.D.

Melvin Scharfman

Melvin A. Scharfman, M.D.
Great Neck, NY

Melvin Scharfman  

This book is one that is focused on a subject of considerable recent and current interest to which psychoanalytic contributions have been very limited. The author, a child analyst, spent a number of years as principal investigator in child abuse for the NIMH at the Downstate Medical Center. This book attempts to extract from that experience and to present a dynamic understanding of the many facets of child maltreatment as well as attempting to learn appropriate strategies for its prevention and treatment. It is, as the title indicates, a handbook for mental health and child care professionals, and it is not specifically addressed to psychoanalysts.

218

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bloomsbury/freud: The Letters of James and Alix Strachey, 1924-1925

Edited by Perry Meisel and Walter Kendrick. New York: Basic Books, 1985, 343 pp. + Index, $21.45.

Aaron Esman M.D.

Aaron Esman

Aaron H. Esman, M.D.
New York, NY

Aaron Esman  

One of the most striking features of the psychoanalytic movement was its impact on the intellectual world—the world beyond the sphere of psychiatry and the field of clinical concerns. Nowhere was this appeal to the intellectual community more powerful than in England—and particularly in that tight, incestuous, erudite, polymorphously-perverse, Cambridge-oriented, and rarified world known as Bloomsbury. Ever in search of new ideas and new cultural passions, the little circle of the Bells, the Stracheys, the Keyneses, the Stephenses and their friends, acolytes, and hangers-on were quickly drawn into the excitement generated by the early reports from Vienna, and Freud soon joined Diaghilev, Picasso and Proust as exemplar of the avantgarde spirit that pervaded the Bloomsbury atmosphere.

James and Alix Strachey were central figures in Bloomsbury. James, the younger brother of the already famous Lytton, drifted into psychoanalysis via a post-Cambridge career in literary journalism. After their marriage in 1920, they went to Vienna so that James could be analyzed by Freud; when Alix suffered a panic attack at the opera, she succeeded in having Freud undertake to analyze her as well. When Freud asked them to translate "A Child Is Being Beaten," he set them off on a career that proved pivotal for the history of psychoanalysis in the English-speaking world—concluding with the monumental achievement of the Standard Edition.

The present volume contains the correspondence that passed between James and Alix during a year of separation occasioned by Alix's decision—prompted by Freud—to seek further analysis with K. Abraham in Berlin. Since both were literate, witty, perceptive, and dedicated people, their letters provide a fascinating personal window on the psychoanalytic world in London and Berlin in the early 1920's—a world in which psychoanalysis in Britain was achieving institutional structure while that in Berlin was caught up in the cultural and political turmoil of the Weimar era. Great names pass in review in these letters—E. Jones, E. Glover, D. W. Winnicott (James's first analytic patient) in London; H. Sachs, S. Rado, L. Andreas-Salome, F. Alexander, O. Fenichel in Berlin—and Melanie Klein in both.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intimität Und Soziales Leid: Archäologie Der Psychoanalyse

By Alfred Lorenzer. Frankfurt am Main: Fischer, 1984, 222 pp.

Sander Gilman Ph.D.

Sander Gilman

Sander L. Gilman, Ph.D.
Ithaca, New York

Sander Gilman  

There seems to be a growing, intense fascination among professional psychoanalysts with the historical context of psychoanalysis. This fascination may well have started with Michel Foucault and his work on madness two decades ago, but it has become centered in recent years on the historical figure of Sigmund Freud.

Alfred Lorenzer, perhaps the most sophisticated psychoanalytic theorist now writing in the Federal Republic of Germany, has now turned to the history of psychoanalysis. He has produced a work which reveals his depth as a psychoanalyst, but many of the weaknesses of the amateur historian. Lorenzer, whose major contribution to the theory of the psyche has been his extraordinary work on the nature of the psychoanalytic symbol, treats in this volume the symbolic function of suffering within the realm of the treatment of mental illness. This book, originally a series of lectures held at the University in Frankfurt during the winter of 1982-1983, presents a teacher's attempt to translate the history of mental illness into palatable form for his students. The central motif, the nature of suffering and its function, is a good one, because it provides a cultural context for the clinical procedures developed to deal with the suffering of mental illness. Suffering is seen as a reality about which society constructs a disease. Each age and culture understands suffering differently and, therefore, proposes alternative means of dealing with it.

While this central thesis is convincing, the material Lorenzer brings to prove it is, I am afraid, quite old-hat. Lorenzer has read Foucault (and acknowledges him in his subtitle), Henri Ellenberger, and Frank Sulloway. He agrees with Ellenberger, disagrees with Sulloway, and accepts Foucault's approach. However, he borrows his historical materials directly from these three sources (and other recent histories of madness such as that by Klaus Dörner) without much thought to the differences among these authors' presentations.

[[RunningHead]]

Bereavement: Reactions, Consequences and Care

Edited by Marian Osterweis, Frederic Solomon, and Morris Green. Washington, D.C.: Nat. Academy Press, 1984, 312 pp., $19.50.

Richard Friedman M.D.

Richard Friedman

Richard C. Friedman, M.D.
New York City

Richard Friedman  

This is a monograph prepared by the Committee on Health Consequences of the Stress of Bereavement of the Institute of Medicine. This multidisciplinary committee was appointed at the request of the Office of Prevention of the National Institute of Mental Health to

828

study the process of bereavement and was cofunded by the Kenworthy-Swift Foundation. The 21-member committee, chaired by Morris Green, M.D., Lesh Professor and Chairman, Department of Pediatrics, Indiana University School of Medicine, included members of the Institute of Medicine, and additional members selected from the general academic medical and scholarly community—all widely acknowledged experts in the field. In addition, contributions to the book were made by consultants in specialized areas. (The Institute of Medicine was chartered in 1970 by the National Academy of Sciences for the purpose of examining public health policy matters.) The committee was instructed to review research on health consequences of bereavement; to outline especially important research to pursue in the future, to make recommendations concerning interventions that might be generally adopted in health care systems; to identify interventions which might not be of proven efficacy, but which should be promptly tested for their value.

Bereavement: Reactions, Consequences and Care is the final report of the committee's study. The volume is divided into three major sections: Bereavement: Reactions and Consequences includes chapters on epidemiologic perspectives on health consequences, adult, adolescent and childhood reactions to bereavement, and various types of bereavement (i.e., death of a child, death of a spouse, bereavement following suicide). Perspectives on Bereavement includes chapters on the biological aspects of grieving, monkeys' responses to separation and loss, sociocultural influences on the bereavement process. Assisting the Bereaved includes the role of health care professionals and institutions, and intervention programs. In addition to reviewing the literature, the committee made site visits to intervention programs in Washington, including: The Children's Hospital National Medical Center Emergency Room, and Neonatal ICU; The St. Francis Center; and The Hospital for Sick Children. Site visits were also made to four other facilities including: The Center for Preventive Psychiatry, White Plains, N.Y.; The Center for the Study of Neuroses (Langley-Porter Institute), San Francisco; Boulder County Hospice, Boulder, Colorado; and Palliative Care Service Royal Victoria Hospital, Montreal. A detailed summary of the site visits is available from the National Academy Press, 2101 Constitution Ave., N.W., Washington, DC 20418.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalyse: Ein Handbuch in Schlüsselbegriffen [psychoanalysis: A Handbook of Key Concepts]

Edited by Wolfgang Mertens. Munich, Vienna, Baltimore: Urban & Schwartzenberg, 1983, 346 pp., Kr. D. M. 48.

Ernst Lurssen M.D.

Ernst Lurssen

Ernst Lurssen, M.D.
Berlin, West Germany

Ernst Lurssen  

842

Wolfgang Mertens is known in the field of both introductory and searching surveys of contemporary psychoanalysis. In 1982 he published Psychoanalysis, an introduction to the subject with special emphasis on the theory of narcissism and the narcissistic disorders. In the same year he edited New Perspectives in Psychoanalysis, devoted primarily to modern developments in metapsychology.

The book here under review is an attempt to provide a clear account of the development of psychoanalysis through the use of particular key concepts. For example: approaches to psychoanalytic theory; basic psychoanalytic concepts; psychoanalytic disciplines (developmental psychoanalysis, psychoanalytic personality theory, psychosomatic theory, education, research on psychosis); themes related to psychoanalytic treatment; clinical psychoanalysis; applied psychoanalysis.

The survey is not exhaustive, but it provides a good overview of current trends, even where these are to some extent contradictory. A short and useful introductory bibliography is presented after each chapter. The book will thus be of interest not merely to beginners but also to more advanced readers. Of particular interest is the implicit presentation of a fragment of the history of psychoanalysis in the Federal Republic of Germany. Although the overview may not be complete, it nevertheless makes clear which authors have especially influenced the theoretical and clinical development of psychoanalysis in Germany, and which trends have predominated. To be sure, certain dangers become evident in those sections where the authors are less concerned with psychoanalysis and its applications than with underpinning political tendencies, for instance, under the catchphrase, "Psychoanalysis and Feminism," evincing the somewhat uncritical politicalization of psychoanalysis of the Frankfurt school.

Aside from this, the book offers a highly original survey of psychoanalysis and is, on the whole, of an even quality in spite of having been written by various authors. In my opinion a translation would be desirable; certainly the book offers an important foundation for those who are interested in the reception afforded psychoanalysis in Germany and in the differences in psychoanalytic practice in various countries.

responseSet.title: Reification Revisited:  Some Neurobiologically Filtered Views Of Psychic Structure And Conflict
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reification Revisited: Some Neurobiologically Filtered Views Of Psychic Structure And Conflict

Andrew Schwartz M.D.

Andrew Schwartz

Andrew Schwartz, M.D.
Barton Hall St. Elizabeths Hospital
Washington, DC 20032

Andrew Schwartz  

Adducing data and concepts from neuroscience, experimental psychology, ethology, and clinical psychoanalysis, the author proposes that (1) one may today move away from traditional but epistemologically troublesome views of "psychic conflict" as "clash" between essentially metaphorical but reified "mental forces" and "structures"; (2) one can now posit instead, a model featuring representational processes, dysphoric affects, and associative mechanisms-all of which, at a neurophysiologic and anatomic level, are indeed "real things" deriving from genuine "structures" in brain circuitry. Furthermore, the centrality of associative conditioning paradigms both in Freud's thinking and in current neurobiologic studies of learning allows one to use additional laboratory findings and derivative ideas to clarify such analytically significant issues as the crucial role of aversive experience in development of neurosis, the fundamental similarity of "drive"-focused and "self"-related or "narcissistic" "conflict," and te increasingly questioned and questionable status of "repression" as phenomenon and as pathogenic agent.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Adolescence and Developmental Breakdown: A Psychoanalytic View

By Moses Laufer and M. Eglé Laufer. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1984, xiv + 225 pp., $20.00.

John Schowalter M.D.

John Schowalter

John E. Schowalter, M.D.
New Haven, Connecticut

John Schowalter  

795

There is a long history of controversy in the psychoanalytic community with regard to the efficacy of the treatment of adolescents, especially young adolescents. There is, however, no controversy about the fact that when treatment is attempted it is often difficult and fraught with problems. For those of us who accept the challenge, new thoughts, whether on theory or technique, are always welcome. In this book Moses and Eglé Laufer describe their views on developmental breakdown in adolescence. Their credentials are outstanding. For two decades Moses Laufer has led a group in London that provides a walkin community service for disturbed adolescents. Evaluation and treatment are provided as needed. Most impressive is that psychoanalytic treatment is available to adolescent patients who could obtain it in no other way. The authors treat a severity of patients usually not available to or accepted by other analysts. Much has been learned at the Centre for Research into Adolescent Breakdown; this book is the result.

The Laufers still struggle with what differentiates breakdowns from depressive psychosis and schizophrenia, but state the former do not always become the latter. They understand breakdown "as part of the adolescent's reaction to being overwhelmed by fantasies containing both sexual and aggressive wishes related to his own body, fantasies which are contrary to the person's idealized body image" (p. xiii). With puberty and its increased biological, psychological and social demands, a break with reality and/or an attack on the body may be necessary for an adolescent to maintain the original distortion of the past that served to maintain psychic equilibrium during latency.

The Laufers stress the developmental aspects of adolescents' breakdown. They see it as necessarily causing a passive relationship to the parent of the same sex and a fixation of percepts of infantile sexuality. They also see adolescence as the developmental phase during which treatment for breakdown is essential. Although treatment is difficult, if it is not obtained or is not successful, they believe there is a foreclosure in the developmental process that usually renders the pathology irreversible.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Dialogue

By Stanley Leavy. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1980, 121 pp., $18.50.

Lila Kalinich M.D.

Lila Kalinich

Lila J. Kalinich, M.D.
New York City

Lila Kalinich  

The word essay comes from its equivalent in French, essai, which means quite literally "a try." In this sense, Stanley Leavy's The Psychoanalytic Dialogue is an essay at its best. It is an attempt to confront more than 20 years of the author's clinical experience and to articulate its meaning. It is an effort to integrate the range of intellectual influences, from F. de Saussure and M. Heidegger to P. Ricoeur and J. Lacan, which have shaped his thought. Leavy avails himself of the reader's attention to provide an opportunity similar to the one he believes to be at the heart of the psychoanalytic situation: to put into words that "yet-to-be-spoken statement" which exists prior to speech, so that desire and intention can be deciphered. To find the right words to say what you mean to a "hearer" is the condition of dialogue. Through dialogue, and dialogue alone, can meaning be disclosed.

American psychoanalysis owes Stanley Leavy a debt of gratitude. His interest in linguistics and linguistic models of psychoanalysis has sown a literature which otherwise might have been somewhat barren in this area. In addition to a wealth of work on Lacan, he has written on C. Pierce, F. de Saussure, and N. Chomsky; on V. Rosen, M. Edelson, and R. Schafer.

810

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Criticism: Theory in Practice

By Elizabeth Wright. London: Methuen, 1984, 208 pp., $22.00.

Ellen Spitz Ph.D.

Ellen Spitz

Ellen Handler Spitz, Ph.D.
Larchmont, New York

Ellen Spitz  

This fine, slender, and concisely written volume presents a kaleidoscope of psychoanalytic perspectives on literature and is highly recommended to psychoanalysts (and others) who wish to acquire an overview of this increasingly esoteric field. It should prove especially useful to American analysts who have heretofore found themselves baffled by the array of continental contributions that tend, when approached from the viewpoint of ego psychology, to blur and recede into mists of confusion. A cautionary note to the casual reader: highly condensed and packed, the agenda of this book is extremely ambitious.

818

Psychotherapy Research: Where are We and where should We Go? (Proceedings of the 73rd Annual Meeting of the American Psychopathological Association, New York City, March 3-5, 1983)

Edited by Janet B. W. Williams and Robert Spitzer. New York: Guilford Press, 1984, 424 pp., $45.00.

Lester Luborsky Ph.D.

Lester Luborsky

Lester Luborsky, Ph.D.
Philadelphia, PA

Lester Luborsky  

Psychotherapy research has matured in the last three decades. The field's coming of age is shown by its greater sophistication in research designs and by the growing body of mutually supportive and recurrent findings among newer and older studies. This book covers in 24 chapters a selection from the great variety of relatively recent studies. The selection is fairly representative except for a skimpiness from among those from psychoanalytic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Observing the Erotic Imagination

By Robert J. Stoller. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1985, xi & 228 pp., $22.00.

John Gedo M.D.

John Gedo

John E. Gedo, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

John Gedo  

From a combination of half a dozen articles published in psychiatric journals, another in a French analytic forum, a new case presentation, and a number of deft essays to bridge the gaps, Stoller has fashioned a provocative and gracefully written book about some of the most puzzling unsolved problems of psychoanalytic psychology. Much of the work was primarily aimed at a readership steeped in biological reductionism, and Stoller used the topic of homosexuality convincingly to demonstrate that human behavior is (almost invariably) mediated through networks of subjective meaning. In the context of this book, the same arguments serve Stoller to remind psychoanalysts that the postulation of various libidinal types amounts to a biological reductionism all the more blameworthy because it makes use of untested biological concepts.

Whatever else he may be trying to say, Stoller has produced a devastating polemic against the dogmatic misuse of theory, the obfuscatory functions of unnecessary jargon, and the illegitimate construction of fictive entities through overgeneralization from limited samples. He has done this with wit and incisiveness and without throwing out the psychoanalytic baby with the methodologically unclean bathwater. To put this even more strongly, he has managed to write a psychoanalytic book that does not fall into any of the errors it castigates.

Stoller's most essential thesis is that, as a discipline, psychoanalysis knows very little about erotic life. Although he demonstrates this most cogently with regard to our putative understanding of homosexual and perverse behaviors, he also makes a good case for the possibility that our assumptions about heterosexual and nonperverse behaviors may be simplistic, based on disavowal of our ignorance through the invention of labels.

I believe that Stoller is right to point out that, if individual case studies suggest that some people become sexually excited when they manage to vomit or to put on a pair of Levis, we cannot take for granted that it is clear what others find exciting about fellatio, coitus, male or female sexual organs, the human body as a whole, love, hatred, or any other object or experience. He is also on the mark when he states that answers to crucial unsolved problems of this kind will only be found if and when we begin to make truly careful observations in an ethological mode, instead of relying on deductions from the discredited hypotheses of our predecessors.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Anxious Pleasures: The Sexual Lives of an Amazonian People

By Thomas Gregor. Chicago: Univ. Chicago Press, 1985, 224 pp., $19.95.

Theodore Lidz M.D.

Theodore Lidz

Theodore Lidz, M.D.
New Haven, Connecticut

Theodore Lidz  

In his aptly titled book, the author provides a study of the sexual lives of the people of Mehinaku, a village of the Amazonian Xingu tribe. The well-written and readily readable book holds considerable significance for psychoanalysts as well as anthropologists, particularly for the following reasons. The book portrays a small society that not only permits extramarital promiscuity to an extent perhaps never before recorded, but the promiscuity promotes rather than disrupts the societal integration. It records how, despite the striking male dominance and the multiple liaisons in which the men are engaged, the men have an underlying fear of women and their sexuality. This work attributes the anxieties, as have other recent studies, to men's residual core feminine identity derived from the child's primary identification with the mother. Another reason, which Gregor does not emphasize or fully appreciate, is the marked similarity of a number of rather unusual beliefs and practices of these South American Indians to those of various tribes in Papua New Guinea—two peoples about as geographically remote from one another as possible, and from such different cultural lines that an explanation of the similarities by cultural diffusion would have to go back at least 25,000 to 30,000 years, almost to the evolution of Homo sapiens.

The Mehinaku are extremely open about sexuality, both in behavior and discussion. Little is hidden from children who may follow a parent out of the village to watch the sexual act; recount, not without pride, the number and identity of a parent's lovers; and themselves engage in intercourse some years before puberty. Marriage supposedly provides exclusive sexual rights to the partners, but both men and women soon engage in an extensive network of liaisons. Husbands are usually only possessive and jealous early in the marriage.

775

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis: The Vital Issues. Vol. I: Psychoanalysis as an Intellectual Discipline

Edited by John E. Gedo and George H. Pollock. Madison, Conn.: Int. Univ. Press, 1984, 436 pp., $35.00.

Allan Compton M.D.

Allan Compton

Allan Compton, M.D.
Los Angeles, California

Allan Compton  

The hallmark of this volume is energy—not "psychic energy" (which is a bête noire in the content of the book)—but an energy of purpose, excitement about a project and prospect. Both the commendable and not so commendable qualities of the book derive from this characteristic.

791

A conference celebrating the 50th anniversary of the Chicago Psychoanalytic Institute, titled "The Vital Issues," was held in November, 1981. This book represents half of a truncated record of the conference, topically rearranged for publication by the editors. They explain that "… many of the most outstanding contributors to the symposium whose presentations focused on psychoanalytic theory could not make their presentations available to us" because of prior publishing commitments (p. xiv). A companion volume will contain the more clinical contributions to the conference.

Psychoanalysis as an Intellectual Discipline is divided into section on: "The Origins of Motivation," "Interdisciplinary Problems," "Creativity and the Life Course" and "Epistemology of Psychoanalysis." There are fourteen papers, four section introductions, and five commentaries or responses. The introductions are the most energetic and in some ways the most interesting parts of the book. Those by Janet Rocah (motivation) and George Pollock (life course) are admirable, the former for its precision, the latter for the effectiveness of its appeal. While it is hard to see much that is particularly psychoanalytic in George Moraitis's section on interdisciplinary problems, his own paper "The Two Readings of 'The Education of Henry Adams'" is highly rewarding. This came as a surprise: I avoided the paper until I had finished reading the others because of my long-standing disinterest in Henry Adams and his "education." Moraitis in this paper is erudite and methodologically sophisticated. The paper is a model for the application of psychoanalysis to works of literature and also offers a valuable prototype of a working method striving toward true interdisciplinary collaboration.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inconscient Et Réalité

By Maurice Dayan. Paris: Presses Univ. France, 1985, 476 pp., Fr. 185.

Patrick Mahony Ph.D.

Patrick Mahony

Patrick Mahony, Ph.D.
Outremont, Quebec
Canada

Patrick Mahony  

I shall begin by briefly giving a background that is not immaterial. Jean Laplanche, the co-author of Language of Psychoanalysis, founded a center of psychoanalytic research at the Université de Paris VII, which already had been known for its interdisciplinary openness. Within the center's program of study and under the direct supervision of Laplanche, Maurice Dayan wrote his doctorate dissertation on the relation between the unconscious and reality. I should explain to a North American audience that Dayan's doctorate is actually a Doctorat d'État, the highest of several kinds obtainable in France and one ordinarily requiring a decade or more to be completed (in Dayan's case, nine years). In point of further illustration: the 476-page tome under review contains but two parts of the original thesis; the third part, Les Relations au Réel dans la Psychose, was published under separate cover, in the same year.

At the center of Dayan's concern is that problematic issue in Freud's metapsychology whereby the unconscious is negatively characterized by its absence of regard for reality, and yet, given the submission of unconscious processes to the principles of pleasure and unpleasure, the unconscious is positively driven to substitute external reality by psychic reality.

1084

responseSet.title: On the Writings of Arnold M. Cooper
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Writings of Arnold M. Cooper

Irwin Hirsch Ph.D.

Irwin Hirsch

Irwin Hirsch, Ph.D.
1311-1327 Lexington Avenue
New York, NY 10128

Irwin Hirsch  

June 22, 1987

In a series of recent articles and addresses including the one in this Journal (35:77-98), Dr. Cooper has been attempting to integrate previously divergent psychoanalytic theories. He is one of few classically trained analysts who publicly sees strong contemporary relevance in the work of the interpersonal school as well as the British object relations theorists and the American self psychologists. His sympathetic

564

attitude toward recent contributions to the understanding of the key concept of transference is an example of this.

The handling of transference is emblematic of how the analyst acts with the patient and how one acts reflects one's belief system. As Sandler (1983) points out, every analyst's true theory is seen in his/her private actions with his/her patients. The way transference is viewed not only reflects one's technique, but one's whole model of the mind. Following Sandler, there may be differences between analysts' private actions and public declarations. The position one takes vis-à-vis transference will reveal whether one is truly a classical, blank-screen analyst; an interpersonal, participant-observer; an object-relational, container, or a self psychologist, self-object.

My second comment relates to some difference in my view of what distinguishes the "historical" and "modernist" views of transference. As Cooper delineates this distinction, the separation between the two views is not sufficiently clear. The modernist point of view, most clearly characterized by Wolstein (1954), Gill (1982), and Hoffman (1983) not only sees transference as a new experience, but views psychoanalytic change largely as a function of a new experience between patient and analyst. Transference is indeed seen as a repetition of the old relationships, just as it is in the historical view. The past is always seen as influencing the present. It is the concept of distortion that is challenged. From this perspective called modernist, the analyst can never know if any given perception of the patient is part of the past or a plausible view of the present. In the modernist view the analyst is not the arbiter of reality (Schwaber, 1983) and the patient's psychic reality cannot be definitively called a distortion.

Edited by John E. Gedo and George H. Pollock. Madison, Conn.: Int. Univ. Press, 1984, XIV + 498 pp., $42.50.

Paul Dewald M.D.

Paul Dewald

Paul A. Dewald, M.D.
St. Louis, Missouri

Paul Dewald  

This second volume of papers presented at the 50th anniversary celebration of the Chicago Psychoanalytic Institute is divided into six sections, each introduced with a brief commentary by one of the conference participants. They include: "Transference and Countertransference," "Self Psychology and Its Alternatives," "Outcome Studies," "Theoretical Problems," "Applications in the Literary Realm," and "The History of Psychoanalysis in Chicago." Fourteen of the participants represented in this volume are from the Chicago Institute; seven are from other psychoanalytic centers.

As is characteristic in volumes such as this, the quality of the contributions is variable, and each is essentially an independently standing contribution even though the editors have attempted to group them in a manner reflecting their general subject matter.

For this reviewer the two most interesting contributions in this volume are in significant disagreement with each other. A paper by Heinz Kohut, "Introspection, Empathy, and the Semi-Circle of Mental Health," presented as a plenary address, is one of his last presentations prior to his death. In it he emphasizes his final conceptual implications of self psychology. for Kohut the concept of biological drives is no longer acceptable; instead, he emphasizes an exclusively psychological theoretical system.

[[RunningHead]]

Event Theory

By Irene Fast with Robert E. Erard, Carol J. Fitzpatrick, Anne E. Thompson and Linda Young. Hillsdale, N.J.: Erlbaum, 1985, xi + 180 pp., $24.95.

Robert Galatzer-Levy M.D.

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Robert M. Galatzer-Levy, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Robert Galatzer-Levy  

Clinicians know that egocentricity, concrete thinking, splitting, enactment, unstable self- and object representation, and like phenomena often appear in concert. Elaborated theories about the motives for these operations are familiar to analysts, but details of their mechanisms and ontogeny are not well described in our literature. We work daily with primitive thought processes, but our ideas about the processes themselves have progressed little beyond their description by Freud and the ego psychologists.

Event Theory examines the early development of thought and emotion, extending Piagetian concepts to include areas of psychoanalytic importance. In Fast's theory, experience is composed of the unitary "events," actions in their entirety. Psychological development is the result of the differentiation of elements of events and the integration of those elements into new conceptual entities. The unitary event, infant-pleasurably-sucking-at-breast-while-gazing-into-mother's-face, is gradually differentiated and associated with other events yielding experiences of a self, action, object, and emotion. Piaget demonstrated that the progressive reorganization of experience through such differentiation and reintegration can account for cognitive development at all levels. Fast and her coworkers expand his ideas to include psychoanalytically important realms, providing new and very interesting descriptions of narcissism, primary-process thinking, self and object relations, egocentricity and emotion.

Babies do not behave as Freud's theory of infantile narcissism predicts. They are fascinated by their environment and interact with it from birth. This interest and engagement is not merely the result of physiological distress.

532

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Use of Audiotape Recording in Impasses with Severely Disturbed Patients

Michael Robbins M.D.

Michael Robbins

Michael Robbins, M.D.
42 Fairmont Avenue
Newton, MA 02158

Michael Robbins  

ABSTRACT

The psychoanalytically oriented treatment of patients with severe personality disorders is characterized by impasses and by efforts to resolve them. Patient audiotape recording and review of sessions can be a particularly useful technical parameter in such situations. When judiciously employed, it is unusual, if not unique, in that it selectively amplifies the power of traditional elements of technique as well as some of the constructive elements of the analytic process itself, while reducing the power of some of the destructive elements of the transference. Indications for using this parameter are discussed, case illustrations presented, and hypotheses advanced about its mode of action. No single element of technique is responsible for the success or failure of analysis. The most that can be expected of tape recording is that it help break up the treatment log jam and facilitate further work.

[[RunningHead]]

La Violence Fondamentale

By J. Bergeret. Paris: Dunod, 1984, 251 pp.

Michael Stone M.D.

Michael Stone

Michael H. Stone, M.D.
New York, NY

Michael Stone  

La Violence Fondamentale, Bergeret's newest book, is an exposition, fortified with case material, of the author's views on the basic innate life force and its vicissitudes. The reader will find Bergeret's book carefully reasoned and, as usual, lucidly written—in an elegant style that, quite apart from the impressiveness of the author's arguments, repays the reader's efforts. What follows both summarizes and comments on these arguments.

From the beginning the infant exhibits a kind of force vitale or life force (as it appears to the world of adults), something akin to Bergson's élan vital, at first rather undifferentiated as to valence and character. The notion of valence soon becomes relevant, as we notice the infant recoiling from pain (or else trying to smash the source of it) and moving toward what is pleasurable. Still within the first few months of life, these basic tropisms become further refined, as the infant learns that most of what is pleasurable stems from "mother" (here used as signifying the primary caretaker, who will usually, though not always, be the actual biological mother). One thinks in this connection of Fairbairn's comment that the infant is primarily object-seeking. Later work by John Bowlby and Daniel Stern supports further the notion that the infant is born with (neurophysiological) structures predisposing it to become object-seeking (as mediated, for example, through innate smiling and clinging responses).

240

The Seduction Hypothesis

Robert Furman M.D. and Allen Marans M.D.

Allen Marans

Robert A. Furman, M.D.
Allen E. Marans, M.D.
6112 Durbin Road
Bethesda, MD 20817

Allen Marans  

THE CHAIRMAN INTRODUCED THE PARTICIPANTS and outlined the format for the panel: Erna Furman would begin with "A Review of Freud's Thinking and Some Recent Considerations"; Robert Furman would present "Reflections from Clinical Child Psychoanalysis"; Miriam Williams would follow with "A Clinical Case Presentation Dealing with the Reconstruction of an Early Seduction and its Aftermaths"; after an intermission, Robert Wallerstein would provide a formal discussion of the previous papers and the topic; the meeting would then be opened to the audience for general discussion with the panelists.

Erna Furman began by highlighting the trends of Freud's thinking about the subject of inner versus outer reality of infantile sexual experiences and their effect on neurotic pathology. In 1896, three papers related the roots of neurotic pathology to sexual experiences in early childhood that cause overstimulation of the immature psychic system. They include children hearing and seeing the sexual activities of adults and particularly being subject to direct stimulation of the genitals as well as to perverse practices involving other bodily zones. In the years that followed, however, prompted by further experiences with patients and the beginning of his self-analysis, Freud found that the neurotics' fantasies were not mere replicas of infantile memories. He came to regard these fantasies as complex protective structures which both contained and distorted the actual early experiences and mainly served to ward off the "impulses which arise from these primal scenes."

Freud's well-known letter to Fliess of September 21, 1897, came as no surprise. He had ceased to view the young child's psyche as an instinctual blank and began to devote his investigative genius to the exploration of the drives in childhood and their role in normal and pathological personality development. Psychotherapy in the Third Reich—The Göring Institute

By Geoffrey Cox. New York: Oxford University Press, 1985, 326 pp., $24.95.

Lore Schacht M.D.

Lore Schacht

Lore Schacht, M.D.
Sexau, Germany

Lore Schacht  

This book, the account by a historian of what happened to German psychotherapy under the Nazi regime with the collaboration of German psychoanalysts, is not easy for the psychoanalyst to do justice to. This is not only because the analyst is used to other forms of reconstructing the past and is thus not familiar with the rules and difficulties of collecting this kind of data, but also because the history retraced here belongs to him in a very different way and concerns him intimately. For the German psychoanalyst the added complication is that he or she is personally involved and all too aware that the past deeply informs the present.

224

Patterns of Change

By Laura Rice and Leslie Greenberg. New York: Guilford Press, 1984, 308 pp., $30.00.

John Oldham M.D.

John Oldham

John M. Oldham, M.D.
New York, NY

John Oldham  

209

Although psychotherapy research is not a new endeavor, publications describing this type of research have been on the increase. Studies such as the NIMH-sponsored multi-center collaborative depression studies and the McLean Hospital schizophrenia treatment study, as well as reports such as those of the APA Commission on Psychiatric Therapies, make an ever-strengthening case in favor of the efficacy of psychotherapy as an active agent of therapeutic change. Clinicians would likely say that they have known this fact all along, and that any well-trained therapist spending a few years working with patients would reach the same conclusion. Perhaps so, but not with the persuasiveness necessary to justify such a costly form of treatment to its consumers or to third-party payors, given the confusing array of types of therapies advertised today (as many as 250 forms of psychotherapy have been identified). As important, however, is the need to establish scientifically the validity, or the lack of it, of any form of medical intervention heralded as a legitimate treatment intended to alleviate suffering or illness. Only through ongoing efforts scientifically to validate or discard treatment methods do we advance beyond admixtures of theory, compassionate intuition, and folk remedies.

Patterns of Change is a welcome addition to the psychotherapy research literature. Drawing from works by researchers with such disparate theoretical frameworks as Rogerian client-centered therapy, Gestalt therapy based on the work of Fritz Perls, experiential psychotherapy, and psychoanalytically oriented psychotherapy, the authors propose a "new research paradigm," in which the "central focus is on the client rather than the therapist. It is the client who changes …" (p. 21).

For years, psychotherapy researchers have been grappling with a bewildering array of data. Among the ingredients to be studied are: defining the therapeutic technique to be utilized; developing reliable scales to demonstrate that observers can agree that a defined type of treatment is actually delivered; measurement and assessment of client (patient) response; assessment of the nature of the therapeutic alliance; estimating the effects of nonverbal and affective communications in addition to verbal articulations; and assessment of the degree to which therapy itself serves as an active agent of observed change, in the context of nonspecific relationship factors or all of the events in "the other 23 hours" of the client's life. Rice and Greenberg point out: "Research on differential treatment effects has recently taken a step forward by ensuring that the treatments being compared have actually been administered.

Theodore Shapiro  

De Saussure(1911)inaugurated the structural analysis of language at about the same time Freud began his seminal thinking about the structure of the mind. Nonetheless, common roots in a common Zeitgeist has not led to an overlap of the fields of linguistics and psychoanalysis. Yet each discipline has a knowledge base that should be explored with respect to opening our minds to new findings in each field on the basis of cross-disciplinary investigation.

[[RunningHead]]

Progress in Self Psychology, Vol. 1

Edited by Arnold Goldberg. New York: Guilford Press, 1985, 269 pp., $30.00.

Joseph Lichtenberg M.D.

Joseph Lichtenberg

Joseph D. Lichtenberg, M.D.
Washington, D.C.

Joseph Lichtenberg  

This volume, the first record of the annual conferences on self psychology compiled after Kohut's death, indicates the continuing power of self psychology to interest readers in the vision of theory and technique of Kohut and those who have worked with or been inspired by him. Readers of this compendium, which is based largely on papers presented at the 6th Annual Self Psychology Conference held in Los

243

Angeles in 1983, may wonder what is meant by progress in self psychology. If, as Goldberg says in his excellent preface, "What Heinz Kohut offered us was … simply (and profoundly) a new theory" (p. 63), is progress the explication of that theory by its most preeminent students? Or, is progress case illustrations that provide evidence for the theory's therapeutic effectiveness? Or, is progress addendums or revisions in the theory itself? In fact, Volume 1 contains papers that attempt each of these ways to proceed.

Two presentations of straightforward explications stand out. "How Kohut Actually Worked," by Jule Miller, is a charming, highly readable, informative account of the author's experience of being supervised by Kohut. Miller conveys Kohut's personal style, the kind and type of advice he received from Kohut, and the manner in which Kohut interpreted Miller's patient's material. Miller offers descriptions of what Kohut regarded as fragmentations, mini-fragmentations, gross identification, and transmuting internalizations. He ably illustrates many important technical precepts that can only be inferred from Kohut's own writings: the analyst should take analytic material first in a "straight" manner, as if it means what it seems to mean, rather than assume a complex encoded message that must be manipulated in order to be understandable. The analyst works in terms of relatively broad interventions that follow the main line of the material, and avoids interpreting details such as slips of the tongue or puns in dreams. He does not hesitate to indicate to a patient when he thinks the patient is making progress. If a patient feels deflated, the analyst needs to review carefully his interventions or lack of interventions in order to understand his contribution to the patient's deflation. Miller's tone of reminiscence is supplemented by Charles Strozier's biographic "glimpses" of Kohut.

Parenthood: A Psychodynamic Perspective

Edited by Rebecca Cohen, Bertram Cohler, and Sidney Weissman. New York: Guiford Press, 1984, 426 pp., $30.00.

Calvin Colarusso M.D.

Calvin Colarusso

Calvin A. Colarusso, M.D.
La Jolla, California

Calvin Colarusso  

This book is the outcome of a 1980 Conference on Parenthood as an Adult Experience sponsored by the Michael Reese Department of Psychiatry. The editors present multifaceted material on what is currently known about this subject. Thirty-two authors, many of them prominent psychoanalysts, contributed twenty-five articles of varying degrees of complexity and differing theoretical viewpoints. The book is divided into four sections focusing on developmental, psychosocial, clinical and theoretical, and future perspective issues.

The reader will not find much of a unifying theme in Section I, "Developmental Perspectives," but will discover seven diverse, well-written articles. In "Sociobiological Perspectives on Parenthood" Jeanne Altmann, a biologist who worked with the Amboseli baboons in Southern Kenya, effectively explores the influence of the environment on psychic development and parenthood in adulthood. Her presentation is followed by a stimulating article by E. James Anthony, on "Creative Parenthood." Such parents cook up "… meals from mysterious left-overs like an alchemist … while holding … a crying child securely" (p. 25). Children in every developmental phase identify with such parents and become creative themselves.

799

responseSet.title: Psychic Structure, Function, Process, And Content: Toward A Definition
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Structure, Function, Process, And Content: Toward A Definition

Sydney Pulver M.D.

Sydney Pulver

Sydney E. Pulver, M.D.
111 North 49th Street
Philadelphia, PA 19139

Sydney Pulver  

A good bit of the confusion in the psychoanalytic literature surrounding the terms "psychic content," "process," "function," and "structure" results from unclear definitions. The author suggests that "psychic content" be defined as the items of our experience. A psychic process is a sequence of psychic acts or events that has some impact on psychic content. A psychic function is the goal-oriented outcome of the action of organized psychic processes. Psychic structure is often thought of as organized, patterned, enduring sequences of mental events, but this definition is a static one. A dynamic definition more in keeping with the way "structure" is used by the structuralists would seem to be better: a "psychic structure" is any organized group of mutually related psychic contents and/or processes which carries out a specific function. Some problems arising from the unclear use of these four terms are discussed.

responseSet.title: Anorexia Nervosa: Theory and Therapy—A New Look at an Old Problem
responseSet.authorMast: Pietro Castelnuovo-Tedesco and  Stephen E. Risen
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Anorexia Nervosa: Theory and Therapy—A New Look at an Old Problem

Pietro Castelnuovo-Tedesco M.D. and Stephen Risen M.D.

Stephen Risen

Pietro Castelnuovo-Tedesco, M.D.
Stephen E. Risen, M.D.
1428 Cloverly Lane
Rydal, PA 19046

Stephen Risen  

PIETRO CASTELNUOVO-TEDESCO INTRODUCED THE PANE. By Stating that the subject of anorexia and bulimia currently is attracting great interest both in scientific circles and among the general public. It is also a topic to which psychoanalysis has made important contributions.

He observed that although there are differences between anorexia and bulimia, most analysts prefer to think of them as facets of a broader eating disorder. He presented a historical overview of the eating disorders with case examples dating as far back as the 1600's. He traced an evolution of our current understanding from both a biological and a psychoanalytic perspective. He mentioned the role of unconscious pregnancy fantasies regressively experienced in oral terms, reaction formations against oral-sadistic impulses, failure to neutralize aggression, defects in ego functioning, preoedipal issues in the motherchild relationship, separation-individuation factors, and the influence of self psychology. He raised the question, "Should emphasis be placed on the drive abnormalities, which are the obvious, presenting difficulty, or on the ego defects which, while consistently pervasive, often are less obvious and even artfully camouflaged?"

The first presentation, "The Psychoanalytic Treatment of Anorexia Nervosa and Bulimia," was given by C. Philip Wilson, who stated that there has been evidence accumulating for the past 30 years that anorexia nervosa is a generic term for a psychosomatic disorder that includes the bulimic as well as the restrictor syndromes.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Denver, CO, May 19, 1985.

Panelists: Barton J. Blinder, Martin A. Ceaser, Mark J. Gehrie, C. Philip Wilson; Discussant: John Hitchcock.

responseSet.title: The Representational World As A Substructure Of The Ego
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Representational World As A Substructure Of The Ego

Arnold Rothstein M.D.

Arnold Rothstein

Arnold Rothstein, M.D.
275 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024

Arnold Rothstein  

The representational world, conceived of as a substructure of the ego, is presented from the perspective of the meaning of the term "structure" for Freud. This perspective builds upon Freud's(1923)delineation of processes of "identification" as the "building up of what is called 'character'" (p. 28) as well as his discussion of "'multiple personality'" (p. 31). I explore this perspective in relation to narcissism, masochism, self-destructiveness, and suicide. I suggest that this theoretical vantage point is particularly helpful in facilitating an understanding of processes that "strengthen" the ego in its effort to assimilate those enigmatic trends Freud(1920)conceptualized as Beyond the Pleasure Principle.

responseSet.title: Foreword
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Foreword

Theodore Shapiro M.D.

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro  

THIS COMPENDIUM OWES MUCh to the Chairman of the Program Committee of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Dr. Paul DeWald, and his colleagues on that committee. They searched for a topic for the 75th Anniversary of the American Psychoanalytic Association that would characterize the intellectual and clinical efforts of psychoanalysts during the past 25 years, and decided wisely on Psychic Structure. The papers presented at the panels have been rewritten in expanded form for this volume, and we have added a complementary section to cover specialized areas of knowledge that have an interface with psychoanalysis. The implications of structure as used in psychoanalysis are well spelled out in the sections devoted to development, adult structure as seen from a variety of vantages, and the effects of treatment on structure. The essays bring the reader up to date on what structure means within each framework and how it is employed to make clinical phenomena comprehensible.

However, reading this volume will not convince anyone that the last word has been written about this compelling concept. Indeed, the process of understanding structure is very much alive in continuing debates about its definition, its application, and above all, the clinical and empirical data used to signify its presence. Ample discussion is afforded the central premise that Freud's structural theory is not all of what is meant by structure. Moreover, the structure-function relation of general biology is revised to account for psychological systems considered apart from the brain. The mind as a functional structure has its own rules of organization that may parallel brain function, but has not proved to be isomorphic.

iii

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Understanding in Classical Psychoanalysis and in Self Psychology

Robert Leider M.D.

Robert Leider

Robert J. Leider, M.D.
180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60601

Robert Leider  

Dr. Reed's (1987) paper, "Rules of Clinical Understanding in Classical Psychoanalysis and in Self Psychology," addresses important scientific and educational issues. However, her scientific argument is, in my opinion, seriously flawed, and her pedagogic recommendations are antithetical to a scholarly educational enterprise.

I will first address the scientific thesis of Dr. Reed's paper, "that self psychology adheres to a system of interpretation that is contrary, not complementary, to that of classical psychoanalysis, and that the two systems are incompatible" (p. 422). Dr. Reed commits herself to focus only upon rules of interpretation, and avoids concern with the validity of self-psychological interpretation, or with the work of individual practitioners.

Dr. Reed begins her methodological analysis by examining the interpretation of a dream reported in the case of Mrs. R. (Goldberg, 1978). From this "text," and excerpts from "The Two Analyses of Dr. Z.," (Kohut, 1979), Dr. Reed draws several criticisms of the exegetic method of self psychology. The two major scientific objections are that: (1) self-psychological interpretations are theory-bound, reading elements of theory into explanations; and (2)

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 198836180-183Psychoanalysis and its DiscontentsBy John E. Gedo. New York: Guilford Press, 1984, 209 pp., $20.00.ArnoldH.Modell Arnold H. Modell, M.D.Waban, MA
responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and its Discontents: By John E. Gedo.  New York:  Guilford Press, 1984, 209 pp., $20.00.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and its Discontents

By John E. Gedo. New York: Guilford Press, 1984, 209 pp., $20.00.

Arnold Modell M.D.

Arnold Modell

Arnold H. Modell, M.D.
Waban, MA

Arnold Modell  

John Gedo, who is one of our more prolific psychoanalytic authors, continues to elaborate in this work on his particular view of the psychoanalytic enterprise. A subtitle might have been: How many analysts does it take to change a paradigm? John Gedo approaches the current state of psychoanalysis with his usual wit and irreverence. It may even be pretentious to refer to paradigms in psychoanalysis; perhaps we have come too far to even pretend that we can share a common conceptual language. Other analogies regarding the current state of psychoanalysis are evoked by Gedo's book: contemporary psychoanalysis can be viewed as an assemblage of schools similar to those schools of painting clustered about the artist who demonstrates a new technique and with it asserts a new method of interpreting reality, whilst others, in opposition, retain a neoclassical vision. And there are those analysts, without followers, who have their own personal vision of psychoanalytic reality; they are in effect a one-man school. These divisions may be described politically as left, right, and center, or they may be, as Gedo suggests, divided in accordance with early religious controversies concerning original sin—whether or not one believes in the perfectability of man.

Despite the fundamental theoretical controversies within contemporary psychoanalysis, Gedo has chosen to write a book that for the most part skirts conceptual issues and has as its centerpiece two lengthy case histories of patients who can be described as on the very edge of analyzability. Of course there will be those who will question whether what Gedo has presented is psychoanalysis or a lengthy psychotherapy using a psychoanalytic setup. That type of question I find to be not particularly interesting as it hinges on a completely arbitrary definition. Be it psychoanalysis or not, Gedo has portrayed favorable outcomes in the treatment of very difficult patients, so that there is some need to account for the outcome in terms of a generalizable therapeutic principle. Gedo falls back on the theory outlined by him in Models of the Mind (with A. Goldberg). I find that when a book is overly abstract I yearn for the specific and concrete. In Psychoanalysis and Its Discontents, it is nearly the reverse; it is replete with intriguing and valuable clinical material, but is too heavily weighted in the direction of the concrete and specific. I missed a more systematic treatment of clinical theory.

responseSet.documentRef: Weiss, S. (1988) Developmental Theory and Clinical Process: By Fred Pine.  New Haven:  Yale Univ. Press, 1985, 269 pp., $25.00.. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 36:188-192

responseSet.title: Developmental Theory and Clinical Process: By Fred Pine.  New Haven:  Yale Univ. Press, 1985, 269 pp., $25.00.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Developmental Theory and Clinical Process

By Fred Pine. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1985, 269 pp., $25.00.

Samuel Weiss M.D.

Samuel Weiss

Samuel Weiss, M.D.
Chicago, IL

Samuel Weiss  

Fred Pine is of course best known in the analytic community for his collaborative work with Margaret Mahler, and it is no surprise that this book clearly shows her influence on him. In this work, he offers us developmental theory, based on observational research and child-rearing practice, and attempts to demonstrate why it legitimately belongs in the mainstream of psychoanalytic thinking. He then focuses on the clinical practice of psychotherapy in an attempt to illuminate developmental theory.

Despite a title that emphasizes a particular point of view, the book lacks cohesion. It is apparent that different parts were written at different times, and for different purposes. Parts One and Two offer an overview of the concept of development as well as a creative and often brilliant elaboration of this concept from a theoretical and clinical perspective. There is a presentation of general concepts that is broad in scope and almost too embracing. It is once more an attempt to convince psychoanalysis that a developmental point of view is an essential, not a peripheral, part of psychoanalytic theory and practice, and should be acknowledged to be at least as important as or more important than our genetic point of view. Although the influence of Mahler on Pine is obvious, what is more significant at this point is the apparent influence of Kohut and self psychology on his thinking.

Parts Three and Four focus much more specifically on issues of child psychotherapy and child psychopathology. These sections tend to be how-to in design and often are quite elementary in contrast to the earlier parts of the book.

The whole book is written in a very straightforward manner, without jargon. It is deceptively simple because the concepts themselves are rich and complex in the first half of the book. The second half is another matter. In fact, in Chapter 14 Pine says, "Recently, when I was discussing some of the ideas presented here with a group of beginning therapists …" This part of the book often sounds like an elementary textbook for beginning therapists. It is clear in organization, but it tends to skim the surface by hinting at data and treatment, and thus is only an introduction of sorts. For example, infantile autism, symbiotic psychosis, and childhood schizophrenia are all discussed in toto on one page.

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 198836517-521Dialogues from InfancyBy René A. Spitz, ed. Robert N. Emde. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1983, x + 484 pp., $52.50.J.AlexisBurland J. Alexis Burland, M.D.Bala Cynwyd, Pennsylvania
responseSet.title: Dialogues from Infancy: By René A. Spitz, ed. Robert N. Emde.  New York:  Int. Univ. Press, 1983, x + 484 pp., $52.50.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dialogues from Infancy

By René A. Spitz, ed. Robert N. Emde. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1983, x + 484 pp., $52.50.

J. Burland M.D.

J. Burland

J. Alexis Burland, M.D.
Bala Cynwyd, Pennsylvania

J. Burland  

According to the Preface, this book collects under one cover the major papers of René Spitz in order to make them more available for health care professionals and developmental psychologists. One gathers the idea for the book arose in response to the dedication of the First World Congress of Infant Psychiatry in 1980 to the memory of Spitz. In addition to the 25 articles reprinted (six of them co-authored with various associates), there are brief but informative editorial comments by the Editor, Dr. Emde, setting the papers into historic or theoretical context, a descriptive list of the still available research films made by Spitz, and seven "commentaries" by contemporary researchers and clinicians on Spitz's contributions to their areas of expertise. The volume is a most fitting tribute. I had thought myself well acquainted with almost all of the included articles and expected I would be able to take a nostalgic journey through the volume while focusing primarily on the contributions of Dr. Emde and his panel of commentators; to my pleasure, I found that such a "concentrated dose" of Spitz's writings was, instead, an illuminating and provocative experience.

Part 1, not surprisingly, consists of Spitz's papers on maternal deprivation—the two on hospitalism, the two on anaclytic depression, and the one recounting how autoerotism is dependent on adequate maternal stimulation. These are probably Spitz's best-known works, and therefore hold few surprises for the well-read analyst. Though these articles describe pathological phenomena, the conclusions reached introduce Spitz's concepts of more normal mother-infant interactions. It is striking how his observations and the hypotheses he reached from them presaged those of Mahler, Kohut, and the infant psychiatrists.

517

responseSet.title: Symbolism and Creativity: By Susan Deri.  New York:  Int. Univ. Press, 1984, 364 pp., $40.00.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Symbolism and Creativity

By Susan Deri. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1984, 364 pp., $40.00.

Simon Grolnick M.D.

Simon Grolnick

Simon A. Grolnick, M.D.
Manhasset, NY

Simon Grolnick  

Susan Deri wrote only two books. The first appeared four years after her flight from Budapest in 1940. The introduction to the Szondi Test reflected her years of apprenticeship to Lipot Szondi. The second, and sadly, last book, finished shortly before her death in 1983, concerns interests she had during her entire career as a psychologist and psychoanalyst. Symbolism and Creativity has become the final statement of a highly articulate member of the New York City intellectual psychoanalytic scene. The book is a sophisticated, comprehensive explication of the author's development of what she terms "a generic theory of symbolism."

Deri believed, and I would concur, that many psychoanalysts carry a rather limited knowledge of the developmental aspects of symbolism, the symbolic process itself, and the relation of symbolism to language and nonverbal communication. This is in the context of clinical psychoanalysis being a field that is nursed by Freud's concept of symbols with unconscious referents, a field that by definition takes place within the symbolic arena of the psychoanalytic situation. Perhaps this lacuna in knowledge is not so surprising because clinical psychoanalysis takes for granted the patient's capacity to symbolize, to ego-observe, and to maintain an "as-if" illusion about the transference. Deri's patients (the book is sprinkled with helpful clinical reports and vignettes) seem to have had difficulties in the course of the development of their symbolic capacities, their self- and object differentiation, and the establishment of self- and object constancy. As Deri acknowledges, theory is influenced by one's patients, hence her concern with the various constitutional and environmental factors that lead to the ability to have and enjoy a symbolic world that mediates between the inner representational world and the perceptual world. This concern leads in turn to the necessity of understanding the genesis of the symbol.

203

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narcisse En Quête De Soi (Narcissus in Quest of Himself)

By Nicolas Duruz. Brussels: Mardaga, 1985. 192 pp.

André Haynal

André Haynal

André Haynal
Geneva, Switzerland

André Haynal  

Nicolas Duruz has the unusual good fortune to have had his psychoanalytic training both in French-speaking countries and in the U.S. He is thus better placed than most to review the subject of narcissism,

242

particularly the contemporary literature. His presentation of the four most important systems of thought in psychoanalytic literature—those of J. Lacan and B. Grunberger in France, H. Kohut and O. F. Kernberg in the U.S.—is lucid and authoritative. Furthermore, he does not neglect to examine earlier writers: from I. Sadger, the "inventor" of the concept (from whom Freud "retrieved" it), to O. Rank, S. Ferenczi, V. Tausk, P. Federn, M. Balint, H. Hartmann, and E. Jacobson.

Duruz' analysis is far from superficial, raising a number of fundamental and complex problems: What is the ego? What constant features form its identity? How can changes and modifications be possible? By tracing out the evolution of the concept of narcissism, Duruz helps us reevaluate its relation to the concepts of ego and id, and gives us a grasp of its many ramifications—economic, for example. By taking account of such an extensive field of writing, stretching from C. G. Jung, W. R. D. Fairbairn, E. H. Erikson and H. S. Mahler, right up to cognitive approaches such as J. Piaget's, Duruz succeeds in placing problems into an unusually broad perspective.

It would be virtually impossible to give a resumé of the wealth of ideas contained in this book. It clearly needs to be read with care, and mulled over. The best recommendation one can give is that Duruz renders easy to understand even those authors most noted for their obscurity. One comes to see why the ego, for Lacan, is finally just an illusion and for Grunberger, a driving force of evolution (independent, though bound up with other instinctual forces), how Grunberger uses the "bioanalytic" concepts of S. Ferenczi—an intricate web of thoughts to explore. On the road to these discoveries, Duruz provides an excellent introductory guide.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comments on Correspondence Between Henry Cohen and Frank R. Hartman

Mark Kanzer M.D.

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer, M.D.
16 Sunny Ridge Road
Harrison, NY 10528

Mark Kanzer  

I find it useful to comment on the correspondence between Henry Cohen and Frank R. Hartman on the Specimen Dream (This Journal, 35:525-8) since an article of mine on the same subject appeared simultaneously with Dr. Hartman's in the Kohut memorial volume, The Future of Psychoanalysis(1983). Neither Hartman nor I were aware of the other's article before publication.

My stand is almost completely in accord with Cohen's, but I believe it would be more fundamental to state briefly my own thesis. The day residue would be the desire to consult a doctor about a patient whom we can now say was Anna Hammerschlag, a point on which I would agree with Hartman. In the course of the dream the patient changes from Anna, the rosy widow, to a pale and puffy woman, with Freud making the comment, "I began to suspect that someone else was being substituted for her" (S.E. 4, p. 109). This "someone," as I suggest from the evidence (largely to be found in Schur (1966) also) was Emma Eckstein. Surely, Irma could be could be a condensation of two conflicting lines of thought!

The real problem that disturbed Freud was neither woman, but the almost broken relationship between him and his intimate friend, Fliess, which centered on the malpractice of Fliess with Freud's patient, Emma, still in treatment with Freud. It can be stated with certainty that she was all but forgotten by them! The dream problem was Freud's alleged malpractice (with Anna Hammerschlag) which he solves by shifting the blame to the hapless Oscar Rie. Schur finds the wish-fulfillment here was to exculpate Fliess' malpractice with Emma, with which I agree.

The first act of Freud after he made his epochal interpretation of the dream, and understood it, was to break the silence between himself and Fliess and notify him cheerfully that he would be coming to Berlin for a sinus operation by Fliess, as the latter demanded. There was no more obvious way of declaring his confidence in the offended friend. In the dream, as the patient (Emma) is being examined, Freud feels he is being examined—an identification and first step toward resolving to yield physically to Fliess, even at the risk of his life. This ultimately did materialize.

responseSet.title: Personality Styles and Brief Psychotherapy: By Mardi Horowitz et al.  New York:  Basic Books, 1984, 350 pp., $25.00.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Personality Styles and Brief Psychotherapy

By Mardi Horowitz et al. New York: Basic Books, 1984, 350 pp., $25.00.

Merton Gill M.D.

Merton Gill

Merton M. Gill, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Merton Gill  

This volume continues the admirable struggle to develop systematic techniques for research on psychotherapy on which Horowitz and his group are engaged. The present study attempts to find particular relationships between personality styles and the technique required to treat psychopathology in the context of such personality styles in brief psychotherapy.

The personality styles dealt with are labeled hysterical, more severe hysterical, obsessional, narcissistic, and borderline. The brief therapy is prelimited to 12 sessions. As a continuation of the group's development of brief psychotherapy to deal with a specific recent stress, all patients were suffering from an unsuccessful attempt to deal with the relatively recent loss of a parent. Where the book succeeds is in its careful delineation of a systematic way of describing a case as it presents, as the therapy proceeds, and in terms of outcome. The method involves a phenomenological description, then a delineation of states of mind, role relationships, and information processing. States of mind refers to relatively discrete recurrent psychic positions, role relationships to self and object relations, and information processing to major themes and styles of dealing with conflict. These three dimensions are successively examined at initial presentation, during several stages of therapy, and at outcome. The three areas of data examination have been described in greater detail in the earlier books by Horowitz and his colleagues. The case presentations are detailed, often hour by hour, clear, thoughtful, sophisticated, and include verbatim extracts. The sessions were video- and audio-taped. As the authors correctly say, the descriptions might well serve as a model for the presentation of longer psychotherapies and even psychoanalysis.

Where the book fails in its avowed intention is in demonstrating a significant particularity in the techniques required by the allegedly different personality styles. If there is any real distinction, it is in global degree of pathology in relation to which the authors show an admirable sensitivity and tact in dealing with vulnerabilities. I was especially struck by the clear effort to make transference interpretations to the extent each case permitted. In other words, in my view the treatment could have been converted into an analysis if desired.
