responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Relationship of Models of the Mind to Clinical Work: Self Psychology

Arnold Cooper M.D. and Samuel Perry M.D.

Samuel Perry

Arnold M. Cooper, M.D.
Samuel W. Perry, M.D.
525 East 68th Street
New York, NY 10021

Samuel Perry  

THE PURPOSE OF THE PANEL WAS succinctly identified in Cooper's introductory remarks. He explained that over the past several years psychoanalysis has benefitted from lively debate over different models of the mind used to understand human development, intrapsychic conflict, and case material. These debates have generated both excitement and controversy; but whereas the models and their associated theories have been well described, the specific technical application of these models has not been sufficiently examined. To paraphrase Cooper, we know much more about what different analysts think than about what they actually do with their patients.

Hoping to fill this gap in our knowledge, the Program Committee organized a series of panels intended to illustrate the clinical work of psychoanalysts who follow specific theoretical models. As one in this series, the Committee chose the model of self psychology. As Cooper indicated, this choice was most fitting in that self psychology has been responsible for much of the current effort in analysis to clarify our theories and our clinical work. Tolpin, a proponent of the self psychology model, generously agreed to present a case and distribute process notes, then have others representing various models discuss the material and the analyst's interventions.

The patient presented by Tolpin was a professionally successful man who had entered analysis in his early forties because of chronic failure to sustain meaningful relationships; for example, his unfulfilling marriage had ended after ten years with "an emotional stand-off" and his subsequent relationships had also typically gone flat after an initial infatuation.

Presenter: Paul H. Tolpin; Panelists: J. Alexis Burland, Homer C. Curtis, Jule P. Miller, Allan D. Rosenblatt.

741

responseSet.title: Some Sources for a Slip in a Translation by Freud
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Sources for a Slip in a Translation by Freud

William Grossman M.D.

William Grossman

William I. Grossman, M.D.
45 Oak Avenue
Tenafly, NJ 07670

William Grossman  

ABSTRACT

Among Freud's papers, we find instances in which Freud describes the "psychopathology of everyday life" as he found it in himself and in others. "A Religious Experience" (Freud, 1928) contains examples of both kinds. In addition, this paper contains a slip of which Freud appears to have been unaware. Freud's paper interprets a religious conversion described in a letter written to him in English. In the translation of this letter into German, Freud inserted material that was not present in the original. He mentions another slip he made in speaking about the letter. These slips and some associated details in the paper indicate persisting unconscious conflict. The content of these slips and details points to an association with Freud's childhood anxiety dream reported in The Interpretation of Dreams (Freud, 1900). Freud's associations and discussion of that dream lead to the Philippson Bible of his childhood, which provides additional connections to the paper of 1928.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mind, Brain, Body. Toward a Convergence of Psychoanalysis and Neurobiology

By Morton F. Reiser. New York: Basic Books, 1984, x + 228 pp., $21.95.

John Nemiah M.D.

John Nemiah

John C. Nemiah, M.D.
Hanover, NH

John Nemiah  

In 1895, having published several papers and a monograph setting forth his epoch-making ideas about the psychogenesis of neurotic symptoms, Freud was seized by a raptus of neurophysiological theorizing aimed at providing a neurological basis for the recently proposed psychological mechanisms of symptom formation. The newly discovered fact that the central nervous system was composed of chains of discrete but interconnected neurones gave Freud the conceptual framework for translating psychology into physiology. In particular, it supplied a useful neurological explanation of memory, which in early psychoanalytic theory was viewed as a central element in the production of neurotic symptoms.

Freud wrote that the neurones serving as vehicles of memory, "are permanently altered by the passage of an excitation. If we introduce the theory of contact-barriers [i.e., the resistance to the passage of an impulse from one neurone to another at their point of contact]; their contact-barriers are brought into a permanently altered state. And since psych(ological) knowledge shows that there is such a thing as a relearning on the basis of memory, the alteration must consist in the contact-barriers becoming more capable of conduction …" (S.E., 1, p. 300).

Here is a remarkable idea—that experience can produce an alteration in the structure and subsequent function of neurones, leading to memory and learning. It was, unfortunately, an idea that was nearly a century ahead of its time.

237

responseSet.documentRef: Loewenberg, P. (1988) Freud for Historians: By Peter Gay.  New York:  Oxford Univ. Press, 1985, 252 pp., $17.95.. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 36:808-810
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Loewenberg, P. (1988) Freud for Historians: By Peter Gay. New York: Oxford Univ. Press, 1985, 252 pp., $17.95.. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 36:808-810
responseSet.documentRefXML: Loewenberg, P. (1988) Freud for Historians: By Peter Gay. New York: Oxford Univ. Press, 1985, 252 pp., $17.95.. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 36:808-810

responseSet.title: Freud for Historians: By Peter Gay.  New York:  Oxford Univ. Press, 1985, 252 pp., $17.95.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud for Historians

By Peter Gay. New York: Oxford Univ. Press, 1985, 252 pp., $17.95.

Peter Loewenberg Ph.D.

Peter Loewenberg

Peter Loewenberg, Ph.D.
Los Angeles, California

Peter Loewenberg  

The time of naive history which presented to readers and taught students "only" the facts and dates is gone forever. All historians now agree that we must seek the whys and wherefores, the explanations of how things developed in a given way and came to be as they are. Historians do not agree on what kinds of explanations are acceptable, which of the social, behavioral, or humanistic sciences are appropriate as aids to historical research and understanding.

Peter Gay is a distinguished cultural historian of the Enlightenment and the nineteenth century who vigorously proposes that historians turn to and use Freud and psychoanalysis to inform their history. He is committed to the classical Freud. He does not play with divergent psychoanalytic schools and modifications of clinical theory. Rather, he finds the versatility and the awareness of social context historians require in the original works of the master.

Gay's strategy of argument is rhetorical—he constructs the historian's defenses against listening to the unconscious as six Dantean concentric rings of intellectual fortifications. He then penetrates and beats down one by one the objections to employing psychodynamic insights in history. The arguments of common sense, technical difficulty, static ahistoricity, culture specificity, of irrelevancy for social and group behavior, are taken up, worked over, and laid to rest, largely from Freud's own writings.

The reservations about psychoanalysis among the historical guild are many and vigorously argued. The world of the unconscious is subversive of reason and at home with contradictions, ambivalences, and omnipresent conflicts. As Gay proposes: "Yielding to the persuasions of Freud will necessarily force historians to change, often drastically, the way they do history, force them to dispense with prized convictions and to revise favorite conclusions" (p. 212).

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis in a New Context: By Arnold H. Modell.  Madison, Conn.:  Int. Univ. Press, 1984, 294 pp., $32.50.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in a New Context

By Arnold H. Modell. Madison, Conn.: Int. Univ. Press, 1984, 294 pp., $32.50.

Phyllis Tyson Ph.D.

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson, Ph.D.
La Jolla, California

Phyllis Tyson  

While not labeled as such, Psychoanalysis in a New Context represents the author's views on psychoanalytic theory and process which he has

825

set forth over the last 22 years. Heavily influenced by the ideas of Balint and Winnicott, he advocates a shift in emphasis for psychoanalysts. Rather than to focus exclusively on intrapsychic dynamics, a one-person psychology, he suggests viewing psychoanalysis as a two-person psychology. As a two-person psychology the focus becomes the process that occurs between two people; examination is directed to the context of interpersonal relatedness and interaction, the matrix of an object-relations understanding of human motivation and functioning. Modell feels that both traditional psychoanalysis—which focuses on psychic reality—and ego psychology—which believes that "maturation is equated with growing autonomy from the environment, a common confusion between functioning and relating" (p. 5) minimize the contribution of the interpersonal. "Psychoanalytic knowledge arises in the context of a two-person intersubjective psychology" (p. 7); "we are less separate than we wish to believe" (p. 6). Modell places special emphasis on the role of affects, noting that affects, not words, are the essential vehicle for interpersonal communication and the source of essential psychoanalytic data. However, Modell does not suggest that a two-person psychology replace the more traditional views of structure formation and internalization, for he feels the two systems are complementary. Yet, he notes, herein lies a paradox, for the assumptions and theories of each system are incompatible with each other. Examination of the epistemological problems that accompany this paradox lead Modell to discuss some of the basic dilemmas of psychoanalysis—the paradox between the intrapsychic and the interpersonal, between the one-person and the two-person frames of reference, between hermeneutics and causality, between drive and self psychology. His discussion includes consideration of some of the philosophers who have addressed aspects of psychoanalysis, such as P. Ricoeur, J. Habermas, and M. Polanyi.

While Modell is to be commended for illuminating some of the murky questions of psychoanalysis, science, and philosophy, the wide range of the questions considered interferes with the coherence of the book as a whole. Perhaps this is inevitable in what is really a collection of the author's views on a number of subjects over a span of years, but it is misleading to present these disparate subjects as a unified treatise. Although some attempt at unification is made by dividing the book into four sections (Defense in a New Context; Therapeutic Action and Psychoanalytic Process; The Problem of Psychoanalytic Knowledge; and a Sociological Postscript) the chapters seem strained in many cases to fit these categories.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Presentations of Gender

By Robert J. Stoller. New Haven, Conn.: Yale Univ. Press, 1985, 219 pp., $22.00.

Eleanor Galenson M.D.

Eleanor Galenson

Eleanor Galenson, M.D.
New York, N.Y.

Eleanor Galenson  

A comprehensive review of the development of Stoller's views on human sexuality and its disorders, and several significant new formulations based on carefully detailed analytic material comprise the major portion of this latest addition to Stoller's already impressive list of publications. In his uniquely rich, expressive, and yet uncluttered style, Stoller summarizes the evolution of his work, beginning with his first clinical encounter with a woman who appeared like a normal male, going on to studies of many patients applying for sex changes, and including 15 cases of boys who showed marked femininity (four or five years old when he first observed them), and 14 families of female transsexuals. This wealth of clinical experience, combined with Stoller's careful analysis of his data, earns him his position as one of the most outstanding contributors to the study of human sexuality.

Following the review of his past work, Stoller tackles the problem of sexual perversions, particularly fetishism, from yet another vantage point, presenting a fascinating account of the analytic treatment of the mother of a three-and-a-half-year-old boy fetishist whose perversity began at age two and a half years or even earlier.

1075

responseSet.authorMast: Robert Michels and  John M. Oldham
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Relationship of Models of the Mind to Clinical Work: Object Relations Theory

Robert Michels M.D. and John Oldham M.D.

John Oldham

Robert Michels, M.D.
John M. Oldham, M.D.
115 East 61st Street
New York, NY 10021

John Oldham  

THIS PANEL, THE SECOND IN A SERIES, was designed to demonstrate the application of object relations theory to psychoanalytic technique with a patient who had a neurotic personality organization. In contrast to borderline patients, where an object-relations approach may result in a novel technique, Kernberg pointed out, neurotic patients can be psychoanalyzed utilizing a similar object-relations framework, in a way that deviates little from the traditional approach of ego psychology.

The patient, Mr. B., was a married businessman in his thirties, father of two, who came for help because of persistent anxiety two months after a serious automobile accident. Prior to the accident, he had been chronically depressed. Mr. B. feared criticism and feared being assertive. His perfectionistic and procrastinating style interfered with his work effectiveness. His marriage was one of mutual affection, though he had difficulty expressing warmth to his wife, and their sexual relationship had become routinized and perfunctory. Mr. B. was concerned about his frequent homosexual dreams and masturbation fantasies, although he did not want nor had he ever had actual homosexual experiences.

Mr. B. was the only child of rigid, pious, hard-working parents who expected him to work, cooperate, and comply with the orderliness of the household. His mother was the dominant figure, insisting that Mr. B. study art, which he did with a combination of resentment and some success. He was a good student, and though somewhat reserved, he had a few friends and dated occasionally in high school. His homosexual fantasies began in adolescence. He met his wife in college, and he hoped his marriage would resolve his secret homosexual preoccupations.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 22, 1985.

Presenter: Otto F. Kernberg; Panelists: Allan Compton, Arnold H. Modell, Calvin F. Settlage, Ernest S. Wolf.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Irrational Executive: Psychoanalytic Studies in Management

Edited by Manfred F. R. Kets de Vries. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1984, XXIII + 497 pp., $20.00.

Richard Munich M.D.

Richard Munich

Richard I. Munich, M.D.
New Haven, Connecticut

Richard Munich  

Since Rice's (1963) seminal work on management, it has become axiomatic that the two critical tasks of leadership are protecting the boundaries of, and generating resources for, the organization or the enterprise which is led. However, the gradual decline of legitimate authority (Schaar, 1970) and the more recent and dramatic downward revision of institutional primacy, have complicated these tasks. When the human factor, in the form of personality style, character, and psychological motivation, is added to the leadership equation, then even systematic thinking and theory building about the topic are difficult. Not the least of these difficulties emanates from the large differences between the language of personality and that of social systems and organizational theory. In addition, much of the psychological contract that an individual makes with his or her organization is unconscious, and many of the expectations of an organization of its members are unspoken. Therefore, issues of irrationality, strain, and dysfunction within the individual and between the individual and the organization are at least as complex as those in any relationship or in any group. Since the fundamental premises of psychoanalysis are subject to debate within the field itself, a study that attempts to go beyond the field is subject to double jeopardy.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Denial and Defense in the Therapeutic Situation

By Theodore L. Dorpat. New York: Aronson, 1985, 294 pp., $30.00.

Ernest Wolf M.D.

Ernest Wolf

Ernest S. Wolf, M.D.
Winnetka, Illinois

Ernest Wolf  

Despite its title this book presents primarily a well thought-out and elaborately argued theoretical treatise, albeit illustrated profusely with

788

clinical case material. The author attempts to bring order and clarity to the welter of sometimes fuzzily defined, sometimes overlapping concepts that describe the psyche's efforts to cope with traumatic perceptions of unpleasure. To achieve his purpose, Dorpat presents an integrated and comprehensive clinical theory of denial and defense. Among the concepts dealt with are repression, primary repression, denial, disavowal, defense, defensive actions, defense mechanisms, projection, introjection, and projective identification. Like most psychoanalysts, he distinguishes "high-level" defenses, such as repression, from "low-level" or more primitive defenses, such as denial. In contrast to most systematic psychoanalytic discussion of defenses that derive their concepts of defensive systems by studying high-level defenses (e.g., repression) and then extend and generalize "from the top down" to all defensive activity, Dorpat proceeds in the opposite direction, "from the bottom up," in developing his theory of defense. He tries to show how developmentally more advanced defenses are modifications of lower-level defensive actions. He postulates denial as the most basic defense and, using both empirical and logical evidence, he argues persuasively that neither the concepts of primal repression nor those of splitting are as useful as the concept of denial in the construction of his theory of defense. He terms the latter "The Cognitive Arrest Theory of Denial and Repression" and devotes the first chapter (of 16) to an expository overview of his theory. In later chapters he elaborates from a variety of perspectives—developmental, interactional, object relations, conscious and unconscious perception, memory pathology, and brain functions.

Dorpat's exposition of his cognitive theory of denial and defense begins with a microanalytic dissection of the concept of denial. He approaches denial as a dynamic process that he divides into four phases, each of which he discusses in detail. The subject's preconscious appraisal of a situation as actually or potentially dangerous or traumatic produces the formation of painful affect not unlike Freud's concept of signal anxiety. This affect triggers a reaction in which the subject turns "focal attention" from what is disturbing, to something else. Not merely anxiety, but any unpleasurable affect such as depressive affect, shame, guilt, anxiety, grief, and helplessness, whether conscious, preconscious, or unconscious, may initiate this turning away of "focal attention" to something else that is less unpleasurable. "Focal attention" is a constructive cognitive process in which the subject seeks to comprehend and represent in words the meaning of what is being attended to. Acts of focal attention exclude the rest of the field from conscious awareness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Adaptive Significance of Destructiveness in Primitive Personalities

Michael Robbins M.D.

Michael Robbins

Michael Robbins, M.D.
42 Fairmont Avenue
Newton, MA 02158

Michael Robbins  

ABSTRACT

Destructiveness in primitive personalities is not restricted to a segment of the person as it is in the case of neurotics, but is global and pervasive. This destructiveness is meaningfully configured in a manner which cannot fully be accounted for by constitutional factors or be comprehended from the individual, intrapsychic, conflictual viewpoint of classical psychoanalysis. It follows an interpersonally meaningful pattern and therefore has adaptive significance in the contemporary life of the individual. The adaptive model I propose does not violate essential canons of psychoanalytic theory, for it can simultaneously be conceptualized in terms of intrapsychic structure and dynamics. This model holds out the possibility for a therapeutic approach to primitive personality organization which differs in some but by no means all respects from classical analysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Theaters of the Mind

By Joyce McDougall. New York: Basic Books, 1985, 320 pp., $22.95.

Elizabeth Auchincloss M.D.

Elizabeth Auchincloss

Elizabeth Auchincloss, M.D.
New York, N.Y.

Elizabeth Auchincloss  

Among French psychoanalysts, one of the most widely read and appreciated by American audiences in recent years has been Joyce McDougall. Alienated, perhaps, by a foreign intellectual tradition which, in the psychoanalytic arena, has tended to focus on metapsychology, stretching into philosophy, American analysts feel comfortable with the work of McDougall, grounded as it has always been on a deep exploration of clinical experience. At the same time, McDougall's theoretical investigations draw from a wide international range of psychoanalytic "schools," so that she creates in her writing a spirit of fresh adventure, unfettered by the chains of any orthodoxy. These qualities, which illuminate her earlier works, are found again in Theaters of the Mind.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Superego Issues

Alan Eisnitz M.D.

Alan Eisnitz

Alan J. Eisnitz, M.D.
262 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024

Alan Eisnitz  

Some questions about superego development are considered, including the distinctions between ego ideal and superego, the separation between ego and superego, the similarity between ego and superego identification, the significance of preoedipal factors, and the important structural gains that result from superego establishment. The organization of the superego is described and important factors in superego development stressed. Among these are the close link between the superego, narcissism, and the instincts; the trend from reliance on external objects to internalization; the need for renunciation of omnipotent bonds with objects, and the capacity for regression within the superego. Clinical examples are provided. The role of the superego in promoting the stability of the self-representation is illustrated.

responseSet.title: The Contribution of Ego Psychology to Understanding the Process of Termination in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Contribution of Ego Psychology to Understanding the Process of Termination in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy

Gertrude Blanck Ph.D. and Rubin Blanck M.S.

Rubin Blanck

Gertrude Blanck, Ph.D.
Rubin Blanck, M.S.
30 West 60th Street
New York, NY 10023

Rubin Blanck  

ABSTRACT

Ego psychology is presented as an integrated psychoanalytic developmental theory, including a theory of object relations. The process of termination is employed as one of the many possible illustrations of the usefulness of this theory. Termination is regarded as a process that pervades the treatment from the outset, rather than as the final phase of treatment only, because the treatment process, whether psychoanalysis or psychotherapy, includes continuous promotion of ever-increasing autonomy. Ideally, by the time termination proper takes place, maximum autonomy has been attained.

To the definition of autonomy as intersystemic, involving relative independence of the ego from the drives (and from the superego), an object-relations dimension is added which extends that definition to include an intrasystemic consideration—namely, relative independence of the self-representation from the object representations. Especially in the treatment of the borderline conditions is the intrasystemic factor cogent because borderline states are characterized by varying degrees of incompletely differentiated self- and object representations. The objective, in the psychoanalysis of neurosis, where self- and object constancy already exist to a large degree, is ego autonomy in the intersystemic sense. In the psychotherapy of the borderline conditions, the objective is greater differentiation of the self-representation from the object representations.

[[RunningHead]]

The Ego Ideal. A Psychoanalytic Essay on the Malady of the Ideal

By Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel (translated by Paul Barrows; Introduction by Christopher Lasch). New York: Norton, 1985, 271 pp., $24.95.

Bennett Simon M.D.

Bennett Simon

Bennett Simon, M.D.
Newton, Massachusetts

Bennett Simon  

As a resident, many years ago, I was embarrassed when one of my respected mentors discovered that I did not know the difference between the terms "character" and "personality." I soon found myself zealously reading the numerous references suggested to me by that instructor, references that left me reverberating with the line from Goethe's Faust, Und bin so klug als wie zuvor ("and I ended up only as wise as I was before")—Faust's rumination about the end result of his scholarly labors. I have more or less come to terms with my intractable agnosia on that distinction, but now, with this book, I reencounter another minor definitional bane of my life: the relation between ego ideal and superego. I had learned early on that the ego ideal is the heir of primary narcissism and the superego the heir of the Oedipus complex. I struggled with understanding the clinical and theoretical implications of this formula, and tentatively concluded that in clinical work the individual variations in appearances, history, and quality of the both superego and ego ideal phenomena were so immense as to render this formula of only the most rudimentary value. Nor could I reliably or usefully distinguish between ego ideal and superego, and most of my supervisors seemed to have little investment in what discussion I tried to generate on that subject. Common sense or introspective psychology tells us that we have aspirations, yearnings to be a certain way or a certain kind of person, as well as prohibitions and exhortations. The psychoanalytic attempt to conceptualize these notions more precisely has been of interest and of significance, especially in uncovering some of the seamy sides of personal morality. But it has not succeeded in creating something much beyond a very rough map and very crude set of instructions, as it were, for exploring, understanding, and occasionally modifying the moral and spiritual pushes and pulls in the lives of our individual patients. The glory of the psychoanalytic theorizing on ego ideal and superego is, to my mind, the incentive to open-ended exploration with each patient, rather than a set of replicably usable results of that theorizing.

[[RunningHead]]

Summary Comments: Panels On Psychic Structure

Jacob Arlow M.D.

Jacob Arlow

Jacob A. Arlow, M.D.
120 East 36th Street
New York, NY 10016

Jacob Arlow  

On the occasion of the 75th anniversary of the founding of the American Psychoanalytic Association, the Program Committee felt that it was appropriate to try the impossible. They asked Dr. Schafer and me to integrate and to synthesize a comprehensive conceptualization of some 21 papers, to say nothing about introductions and comments, presented at three different half-day panels at the Annual Meeting. While such a request may be taken as a compliment, I have to express certain misgivings, primarily because I fear that I may slight certain issues and important presentations made by members of the various panels. I can only assure them that any such oversight is unintentional. I shall try to highlight some of the broader issues and to emphasize a few topics of special interest.

[[RunningHead]]

Changing Psychic Structure Through Treatment: From Empathy To Self-reflection

Arnold Goldberg M.D.

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg, M.D.
180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60601

Arnold Goldberg  

The central thesis of this paper is that psychic structure may be considered as a complex system of varying configuration. This configuration can be a microstructure or a macrostructure such as the ego or the self. The issue of changing structure through treatment involves the patient's expanded understanding. The steps involved in this process are sequentially delineated and illustrated with a clinical example. The crucial step of self-reflection is utilized as a sine qua non of such change.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Development Of The Ego: Insights From Clinical Work With Infants And Young Children

Stanley Greenspan M.D.

Stanley Greenspan

Stanley I. Greenspan, M.D.
7201 Glenbrook Road
Bethesda, MD 20814

Stanley Greenspan  

This paper describes how the ego develops from its somatic-sensory-affective foundation to embrace object relations, to expand into a range of drive-affect-thematic domains, and to organize itself into self-object patterns and structures. It is postulated that the early growth of the ego can be characterized in terms of six levels of experiential organization including early stages of relatively undifferentiated somatic and behavioral self-object patterns and global reactivity, and later stages of differentiated representational experience and selective adaptive and defensive transformations of experience. Characterizing early ego development into stages facilitates understanding of how the ego develops and of the contributions of unique biological and experiential patterns in adaptive and maladaptive development. It is also postulated that each stage of ego development has as part of its foundation constitutional-maturational patterns related to sensory affective processing and integration, as well as haracteristic caregiver-infant and child interaction patterns. The relation between individually different patterns of sensory-affective processing and specific environmental (experiential) patterns is considered. Hypotheses and research strategies are suggested which may prove helpful in studying how biology and experience interact in the formation of psychopathology and of defensive structures.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Writing about Psychoanalysis: I. Analysts who Write and Those who Do not

Martin Stein M.D.

Martin Stein

Martin H. Stein, M.D.
8 East 96th Street
New York, NY 10128

Martin Stein  

ABSTRACT

This study of analysts' writing consists of two parts. In the following paper, I describe the difficulties facing the practicing analyst who wishes to lecture and publish his findings and ideas, whether or not he intends to cite clinical examples. I make some suggestions directed toward overcoming those (largely internal) obstacles. In a second paper, I shall discuss a special problem, concern over how our lecturing or publishing affects the analytic progress of our patients, especially with respect to analysis of the transference.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalysis of Art: Some Ends, Some Means
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalysis of Art: Some Ends, Some Means

Donald Kaplan Ph.D.

Donald Kaplan

Donald M. Kaplan, Ph.D.
175 West Twelfth Street
New York, NY 10011

Donald Kaplan  

ABSTRACT

The psychoanalysis of art has been a lively activity for virtually a century, ever since Freud first likened certain findings of his self-analysis to certain turns of plot in Oedipus Rex and Hamlet. Yet over this time a lack of clarity has persisted with respect to the kind of knowledge applied psychoanalysis achieves and its means of justification. Starting with the observation that clinical and applied psychoanalysis are, in every respect, radically different endeavors, this paper goes on to identify some ends and means of the psychoanalytic study of art and to suggest a few criteria of adequacy for the outcomes of such study.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Dimensions of Masochism

Otto Kernberg M.D.

Otto Kernberg

Otto F. Kernberg, M.D.
New York Hospital—Cornell Medical Center
21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, NY 10605

Otto Kernberg  

ABSTRACT

In this paper, I propose a general classification of masochistic psychopathology and describe relations between this clinical domain and other types of psychopathology. My main objective is to provide an outline relevant for diagnostic, prognostic, and treatment considerations of masochistic pathology. This includes descriptions of and relations among a wide variety of masochistic phenomena from the depressive-masochistic personality to extreme forms of self-destructiveness.

Ego organization, object relations, superego development, narcissistic organization, and polymorphous perverse infantile sexuality are considered as codeterminants of the levels and clinical features of masochistic pathology. Finally, the relations between masochistic pathology and negative therapeutic reactions are reexamined.

[[RunningHead]]

The Four Psychologies of Psychoanalysis and their Place in Clinical Work

Fred Pine Ph.D.

Fred Pine

Fred Pine, Ph.D.
Department of Psychiatry Albert Einstein College of Medicine
1300 Morris Park Avenue
Bronx, N.Y. 10461

Fred Pine  

ABSTRACT

Clinical psychoanalysis has led to the development of four conceptually separable perspectives on the functioning of the human mind. These are referred to herein as the psychologies of drive, ego, object relations, and self. Their clinical relevance is explored by applying them to issues regarding evenly hovering attention and the mutative factors in psychoanalysis. Those two areas, in turn, are seen to undergo an expansion when viewed from the perspective of each of the four psychologies.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>Langley-Porter Institute</ln><ln>401 Parnassus Avenue</ln><ln>San Francisco, CA 94143</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis, Psychoanalytic Science, and Psychoanalytic Research—1986
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, Psychoanalytic Science, and Psychoanalytic Research—1986

Robert Wallerstein M.D.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.
Langley-Porter Institute
401 Parnassus Avenue
San Francisco, CA 94143

Robert Wallerstein  

ABSTRACT

The present standing of psychoanalysis as a science and the vitality of psychoanalytic research effort are reviewed. The two are interdependent, since the possibilities for empirical research rest on the necessary assumption that psychoanalysis is indeed enough a science to be susceptible to knowledge advance by the (research) methods of science. Concerning our status as a science, I review attacks on our scientific credentials (both from within our ranks and without) by the logical positivists, by the hermeneuticists (a rubric comprising a variety of hermeneutic, phenomenological, exclusively subjectivistic, and/or linguistically based conceptualizations of our field), and the most recent by the philosopher of science, Adolf Grünbaum. I try to demonstrate what I feel to be the failure of each of these assaults, and why I feel there is no reason to see psychoanalysis as anything other than a scientific psychology and, therefore, in theory amenable to empirical research approaches. I then review the history and the current status of these systematic research efforts in psychoanalysis, and the reasons why these have been far less in scope and in accomplishment than has been possible or than has been needed. Here I have focused especially on research involving technique and our theory of change and cure—i.e., research on the analytic process; on what changes take place (outcome) and how those changes come about or are brought about (process).

responseSet.title: Reply to Mark J. Blechner
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Mark J. Blechner

Hans Loewald M.D.

Hans Loewald

Hans Loewald, M.D.
2 Baberry Road
Hamden, CT 06517

Hans Loewald  

December 19, 1986

I welcome Dr. Blechner's straightforward and thought-provoking comments on my paper "Transference-Countertransference," in which I have tried to point to an inextricable intertwining of the two. His reaction to the paper is in many respects positive, but he deplores "wandering terminology" and the "recklessness with denotative meanings" of a term, referring to my using the term "countertransference" not only in respect to the analyst but also in respect to equivalent reactions of the patient to the analyst.

I readily admit the inconvenience of using "countertransference" in an unconventional way, and I may underestimate the troublesome nature of that inconvenience. If people come to find it too confusing to think of countertransference of the patient, I would not insist on that usage. But I do believe that the 75-year-old convention, in view of present understanding of transference and countertransference, is an anachronism. Confusion may end in chaos, and confusion may serve as a spur to reappraising and rethinking our terms so as to promote clarification.

557

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Female Adolescence: Psychoanalytic Reflections on Literature

By Katherine Dalsimer. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1986, 141 pp., $16.95.

Rosemary Balsam M.D.

Rosemary Balsam

Rosemary Balsam, M.D.
New Haven, Connecticut

Rosemary Balsam  

It is a rare gift to write about the psychological meaning of adolescent experience without losing the vitality of the young life contemplated. Dr. Dalsimer possesses this gift. Her book is so well written, well conceived, and entrancing that it stimulates the reader's eagerness to dwell again in delight with the adolescent characters in the beloved classics on which her critique is based. The choice of texts superbly fulfills the purpose of studying progressive phases, from the twelve-year-old boy-girl Frankie, in Carson McCuller's The Member of the Wedding, through the clique of high-school girls in Muriel Spark's The Prime of Miss Jean Brodie, the private experience of the mid-adolescent Anne Frank in her Diary, the tempestuous Juliet in Shakespeare's Romeo & Juliet, and finally the twenty-seven-year-old Anne Elliot, who rediscovers the lost love of her late adolescence in Jane Austen's Persuasion.

"Psychoanalytic theory," Dalsimer says, "can enrich the reading of a text …" and "literary texts, in turn deepen our understanding of psychological process." She wants to extend the reader's awareness of the unfolding of female adolescence.

814

responseSet.title: Principles of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: A Manual for Supportive-Expressive Treatment: By Lester Luborsky.  New York:  Basic Books, 1984, xxiv + 270 pp., $18.95.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Principles of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: A Manual for Supportive-Expressive Treatment

By Lester Luborsky. New York: Basic Books, 1984, xxiv + 270 pp., $18.95.

Jerome Katz M.D.

Jerome Katz

Jerome B. Katz, M.D.
Topeka, Kansas

Jerome Katz  

In this volume Lester Luborsky, a highly respected and seasoned researcher, therapist, psychoanalyst, and teacher, succeeds remarkably well in conveying in deceptively simple fashion the core principles of psychoanalytically informed psychotherapy, a definitive description of the technique for carrying it out, a detailed guide to the therapist on the practice of the techniques, and measuring devices to ascertain to what degree the therapist has conformed to the techniques and practice, along with measures and scales for evaluating the outcome. It is the first of its kind. Although there are many books and recommendations on the conduct of psychoanalytic psychotherapy, this manual is the first to meet the strict format requirements of defining as completely as possible recommendations, the main techniques or operations the therapist is to perform, and the scales to measure his performance. The latter is a requirement often neglected. As well, it is a training tool for therapists, supervisors, and teachers. In reducing the complexities of psychoanalytically informed theory and practice to simpler terms, something is lost, much is gained. Luborsky provides the means of validating the efficacy of therapy, something which he himself, along with others, has served to establish. He seeks to articulate a theory of change, to define technique in operational terms so that it can be taught, to provide reliable scales to determine whether treatment has been accurately conducted, and to supply instruments for assessing change.

The present manual is a refinement of previous manuscript versions which Luborsky had been testing out for many years. The core principles of therapy are based on Freud's recommendations for psychoanalytic technique and the development and application of psychoanalytic psychotherapy at the Menninger Foundation, where Lester Luborsky spent 13 years studying classical psychoanalysis and the style of supportive-expressive psychotherapy he describes. While psychodynamic psychotherapy derived from psychoanalysis is among the most frequently employed of the psychotherapies (perhaps 85 percent of practitioners claim to be "psychodynamic" in orientation), it has been the least studied in systematic fashion for its efficacy.

Narcissistic States and the Therapeutic Process

By Sheldon Bach. North Vale, N.J.: Aronson, 1985, 253 pp., $25.00.

Arnold Goldberg M.D.

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold I. Goldberg, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Arnold Goldberg  

Dr. Bach's face beams out at us from the cover of this book, and one is not disappointed in this warm and friendly countenance having a corresponding clinical appeal. This book is filled with clinical vignettes, and the author proves himself to be a sensitive and astute clinician and teacher. His written effort is directed to fostering a "treatment attitude" which will allow for the use of psychoanalysis in the effective amelioration of all manner of narcissistic disorders, and there is little doubt of his success in this endeavor.

850

[[RunningHead]]

Normality and the Life Cycle

Edited by Daniel Offer and Melvin Sabshin. New York: Basic Books, 1984, 460 pp., $29.95.

Calvin Colarusso M.D.

Calvin Colarusso

Calvin A. Colarusso, M.D.
La Jolla, Calif.

Calvin Colarusso  

This book is intended to "… document what we know about normality, or mental health, throughout the life cycle" (p. IX). It is divided into three parts. The first, "The Life Cycle" (Chapters 1–7), describes what is currently known about normality from infancy to old age. This is followed by four chapters on theoretical and methodological perspectives. The book concludes with an integration and expression of their own viewpoint by the editors.

The chapters in Part I are all extremely comprehensive and well written. They certainly achieve the editors' goal of documenting what is currently known about normality. The bibliographies themselves are worth the purchase of the book. In Chapter 1, "Infancy: Perspectives on Normality," Robert Emde and James Sorce discuss infancy from Offer and Sabshin's four perspectives on normality (as utopia, average, health, and transactional system). They find three of the four self-evident and useful, but believe the utopian concept begs for clarification. After reviewing three disciplinary traditions on infancy (the differential psychology-IQ; pediatric-neurologic; and psychiatric-psychoanalytic)

1090

[[RunningHead]]

Narcissisme Et États Limites

Edited by Jean Bergeret and Wilfrid Reid. Montreal: Dunot & Presses Univ. Montréal, 1986, 244 pp., $20.00 Canad.

Yvon Gauthier M.D.

Yvon Gauthier

Yvon Gauthier, M.D.
Montreal, Canada

Yvon Gauthier  

This book brings together some interesting ideas and hypotheses: borderline states are in close relation with, or the expression of, a severe "essential" depression; this "essential depression," thought to be more frequent than in previous decades, is in close relation with the important sociocultural changes of our modern society. It is also an effort at seeing convergent thinking between psychiatrists, more centered on diagnostic attempts to differentiate between borderline states and other pathological manifestations, and psychoanalysts who are more interested in finding a specific place for borderline states within psychoanalytic theory and in applying the therapeutic implications of such a dynamic comprehension.

There is an international component to this effort, since psychiatrists and psychoanalysts of several European and American countries have been asked to summarize their specific contribution on this theme for the concretization of this book. A summary of each contribution might not be the best way to do justice to this common endeavor. I prefer to give a brief review of the main thoughts and hypotheses which are common to the varied experiences of each author.

Anxiety about loss of object love no doubt is the main theme that recurs through most of the contributions to explain borderline states. Bergeret (France) is the one who insists most on this idea and who places it at the core of his thinking. It is for him the prototype of anxiety in états limites, much tied up to his theory of primitive violence leading to the basic conflict: "It's her or me."

For C. A. Amarel Dias (Portugal), his study of borderline adolescents brings him to understand such severe problems in the light of Mahler's separation-individuation concept: mothers do not accept the adolescent's attempt at autonomy, by their anxiety and overprotection, they hinder his normal individuation process.

In two contributions, an important link is made between addiction and borderline states. A. Charles-Nicolas (France) places the emphasis on the insufficiency of internal objects, much related to the mother's inability to let her child develop on his own, and on the release of aggressive instinct which follows. M. Defayolle (France)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How Does Analysis Cure

By Heinz Kohut. Edited by Arnold Goldberg with collaboration of Paul Stepansky. Chicago: Univ. Chicago Press, 1984, 240 pp., $27.50.

Arnold Cooper M.D.

Arnold Cooper

Arnold M. Cooper, M.D.
New York, NY

Arnold Cooper  

Heinz Kohut died on October 7, 1981 after an extraordinary career. Having been the President of the American Psychoanalytic Association and an admired teacher and leader within classical psychoanalysis, he then became the inventor of self psychology and the revered and excoriated leader of this new movement within psychoanalysis that claimed to raise psychoanalytic theory and technique to a new conceptual level: psychoanalysis would no longer be confined to "guilty man," but would be able to encompass the hugeness of "tragic man" as a creative being in an endless universe. He was a charismatic teacher and a mesmerizing speaker. The often acrimonious debate aroused by his work sparked a reexamination of many basic propositions. For example, while Kohut was sharply criticized for advocating cure by kindness and empathy, his critique of standard analysis led us to a careful and often creative reassessment of our concepts of neutrality, the analytic setting, the sources of transference responses, and the focus on intrapsychic experience. Also, his different ideas about the origins of neurosis became part of a renewed debate on the centrality of the Oedipus complex, the role of the "real" environment of development, and the availability of preoedipal experience in the transference.

The book under review was completed shortly before his death and edited posthumously. It is clear that Kohut had multiple agendas for this book. He addresses not only the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis, but he also attempts to redress errors and misunderstandings about self psychology that had arisen both among his followers and his repudiators. Kohut is at pains throughout the volume to make clear that self psychology is a powerful, even revolutionary, new theory of psychoanalysis, while at the same time he gives an unexpected (at least to this reviewer) and unalloyed allegiance to the core of psychoanalytic theory and technique as developed by Freud.

175

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rituals of Manhood: Male Initiation in Papua New Guinea

Edited by Gilbert H. Herdt. New York: Univ. Calif. Press, 1982, 367 pp., $42.00; paperback, $12.95.

Leo Rangell M.D.

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell, M.D.
Los Angeles, California

Leo Rangell  

Psychoanalytic studies have from the beginning dovetailed with those of anthropology, as with history, biography, literature, and every discipline studying the behavior of man. As archeology has not only "fitted in" with psychoanalysis but contributed one of its enduring metaphors, so has anthropology also nestled in comfortably with the goals and spirit of psychoanalysis. All three study and point to an understanding of older forms of current human behavior.

In an imaginative leap, Freud, in Totem and Taboo, speculated from the living individual back to the oldest conceivable forms of human society. Since then, anthropologic studies of living forms of primitive human social organizations have, just as child developmental studies, enabled psychoanalysts to understand and theorize prospectively and developmentally, complementing the retrospective and genetic approaches of the psychoanalytic procedure. Such studies, proceeding from both directions and meeting each other, have gone a long way toward providing further scientific conviction of psychoanalytic postulates.

This book is a collection of papers presented at a symposium of the Association for Social Anthropology in Oceania held in Florida in 1979.

833

Lionel Finkelstein  

ABSTRACT

This paper attempts to indicate why psychoanalysts should be interested in marital therapy. It discusses the clinical advantages and disadvantages of marital therapy as compared to psychoanalysis, describes the contributions of the classical psychoanalytic viewpoint to marital therapy, and finally, attempts to complement the classical psychoanalytic viewpoint with an object-relations viewpoint which can illuminate marital dynamics and provide interpretive content during the course of marital therapy.

[[RunningHead]]

Changing Psychic Structure Through Treatment: Preconditions For The Resolution Of The Transference

Arnold Modell M.D.

Arnold Modell

Arnold H. Modell, M.D.
401 Woodward Street
Waban, MA 02168

Arnold Modell  

The author compared patients who make creative use of the transference to effectuate structural change to other patients whose analyses appear to be interminable. Multiple preconditions were judged to be necessary: we must assume a capacity for symbolic transformation—an ability to shift from fantasy to reality and from reality to fantasy. This in turn requires a capacity to tolerate the ambiguity and paradox of the mutliple realities implicit in the psychoanalytic process, the ambiguity of actuality and illusion, the paradox of time—that the past is in the present and that the present can alter what is past. Finally, the resolution of the transference presupposes a certain capacity to tolerate psychic pain and to resolve that pain through the process of mourning.

responseSet.title: Fantasy or Structural Defect? the Borderline Dilemma as Viewed from Analysis of an Experience of Nonhumanness
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fantasy or Structural Defect? the Borderline Dilemma as Viewed from Analysis of an Experience of Nonhumanness

Melvin Singer M.D.

Melvin Singer

Melvin Singer, M.D.
Institute of Pennsylvania Hospital
111 North 49th Street
Philadelphia, PA 19139

Melvin Singer  

ABSTRACT

The experience of nonhumanness, a crucial aspect of the phenomenal self of borderline states, is studied in two cases to test assumptions concerning the application of the classic ego-psychological or object-relations and self-psychological models. Are these experiences to be seen as fantasy and treated as manifest content, with dynamically active unconscious conflict and meaning, requiring interpretation, or as a developmental arrest in which structural disturbances are ameliorated through clarifying and empathic interventions?

The first case is of a patient with a neurotic depression and hysterical and obsessional character traits, treated in a classic psychoanalysis. Even though dyadic oral and anal fantasies were strongly in evidence, the ego was well integrated.

The second case is of a severe borderline patient who showed depressive panic and agorophobia. Her major ego disturbances, poorly integrated and differentiated self- and object representations, and primitive defenses prevented effective psychoanalytically oriented treatment. Interventions were used to consolidate self-representations, internalize tension-regulating structures, promote adaptive ego functions and reality testing. These clarifications were necessary to move the patient to where she could tolerate facing unconscious dynamic conflicts. Only at that point could repression be lifted, memory emerge, and interpretation be utilized.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and the Hermeneutic Turn: A Critique of Narrative Truth and Historical Truth

Louis Sass Ph.D. and Robert Woolfolk Ph.D.

Robert Woolfolk

Louis A. Sass, Ph.D.
Robert L. Woolfolk, Ph.D.
Department of Clinical Psychology GSAPP—Busch Campus Rutgers University
New Brunswick, NJ 08903

Robert Woolfolk  

ABSTRACT

Through a discussion of Donald Spence's Narrative Truth and Historical Truth, a critical introduction to the hermeneutic or interpretive perspective is presented. Spence's book has generally been assumed to offer a hermeneutic reformulation of psychoanalysis. However, its presuppositions are incompatible with fundamental tenets of contemporary hermeneutic thought, as expressed in the philosophies of Heidegger, Gadamer, and the later Wittgenstein.

Spence's basic assumptions are classically empiricist and positivistic. His vision of human experience is essentially associationistic and Humean; it treats experience as involving two processes, the passive reception of raw sense data and a subsequent projection of meaningful interpretation. Spence advocates the gathering of brute data while denying or downplaying the epistemological value of theorizing and of interpretive understandings. These assumptions are contrasted with those of the hemeneutic philosophers. Unlike these philosophers, Spence tends to dichotomize coherence and correspondence theories of truth. As a result, he wavers between relativism (regarding therapeutic interpretations) and objectivism (regarding scientific knowledge).

The Current Status of Psychoanalysis in Medical Student Education in the United States: A Preliminary Overview

Stuart Asch M.D. and Eric Marcus M.D.

Eric Marcus

Stuart S. Asch, M.D.
Eric R. Marcus, M.D.
525 East 68th Street
New York, NY 10021

Eric Marcus  

ABSTRACT

With the aim of evaluating psychoanalytic teaching in premedical curricula, a survey was made of the psychiatric readings used in a sampling of 12 United States medical colleges. Eighteen of the main psychiatric texts recommended by the schools are reviewed as one way of evaluating psychoanalytic course content.

In addition, the authors present an overview of the problems that confront the teaching of psychoanalytic concepts in medical education. Specific constructive suggestions are offered.

responseSet.title: Discussion Of Panel Presentations On Psychic Structure
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion Of Panel Presentations On Psychic Structure

Roy Schafer Ph.D.

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer, Ph.D.
241 Central Park West (#1A)
New York, NY 10024

Roy Schafer  

My reading of Freud's works shows that he founded his structural theory on the energic aspects of his theories of dreams and drives, and that he never did and, for systematic reasons, never could give up this foundation. Transformations of primary-process functioning are intrinsic to all psychological development. My reading of the panel papers on psychic structure, as originally presented, shows that current usage often departs significantly from Freud's structural-energic propositions and that these propositions no longer strictly control psychoanalytic theorizing and research. In what seems to be a shift toward minimalism in theoretical formulation, modern structural theory is increasingly situated in a different conception of explanation; for example, there is less or no concern with how basic human tendencies are possible in the first place. Emphasis now tends to fall on the development, organization, and function of stable psychic organizations. Especially to be explained are developments in sel-experience, object relations, and adaptation. This theoretical shift is then linked to cultural changes favoring emphasis on process as against entity, subjective experience, individualistic theory construction, and rejection of sharp demarcation of the boundary between the observer and the observed. Examples of these changes are discussed briefly.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Structure Formation: The Complementary Roles Of Affects, Drives, Object Relations, And Conflict

Phyllis Tyson Ph.D.

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson, Ph.D.
3252 Holiday Court (#102)
La Jolla, CA 92037

Phyllis Tyson  

Diversity in psychoanalytic approaches to psychic structure leads to argument over the relative status of self and ego. This paper takes the position that a sense of self emerges and endures because of the synergistic functioning of the ego. Therefore, the structures under consideration are those of the structural model. A complete view of psychic structure formation must consider not only the origin and development of each of the psychic systems, but also their integration within the overall personality. Affect, conflict, libidinal object constancy, and the infantile neurosis are scrutinized as to their roles in the formation and integration of the id, ego, and superego.

responseSet.title: Kernberg and Kohut: A Test of Competing Psychoanalytic Models of Narcissism
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Kernberg and Kohut: A Test of Competing Psychoanalytic Models of Narcissism

Marc Glassman Ph.D.

Marc Glassman

Marc Glassman, Ph.D.
118 West 72nd Street
New York, NY 10023

Marc Glassman  

ABSTRACT

Over the past ten years the problem of validating psychoanalytic theory has received increasing attention from psychoanalysts and nonpsychoanalysts alike. Yet, very little research responsive to this question of validation has been produced. During this same period one of the most fertile controversies in contemporary psychoanalysis reached its peak—the Kernberg-Kohut debate concerning the nature of narcissism. However, for the lack of a validation strategy, this debate has become increasingly sterile, degenerating to a theoretical stalemate at this point.

This paper addresses the problem of validation by using causal modeling as a mechanism for empirically testing many of the claims of these competing models of narcissism. Although the data provide empirical support for both theories, strategic comparisons tentatively suggest that Kohut's self psychology is more parsimoniously explained as a special case of Kernberg's ego psychology-object relations theory.

responseSet.title: On Working Through: A Model from Artificial Intelligence
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Working Through: A Model from Artificial Intelligence

Robert Galatzer-Levy M.D.

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Robert M. Galatzer-Levy, M.D.
180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60601

Robert Galatzer-Levy  

ABSTRACT

Working through is centrally important to clinical psychoanalysis. It is inadequately explained in analytic theory. An artificial intelligence model of the process is proposed. Models of problem solving show that the complexity of necessary computation is an important determinant of how a problem is solved. Not optimal, but only good enough solutions are usually found. The quality of solutions depends on the time and resources available. Generally it is far easier to use existing methods than to develop new approaches. When problems must be solved in an emergency fashion, as in trauma, poor solutions are likely to emerge. In studying the annealing of metals and other complex optimization problems, a process, the Boltzman algorithm, was discovered, which continues the search for better solutions while gradually developing a coherent structure of the overall solution. The algorithm provides a model both for psychoanalytic working through and for the normally ongoing process of psychological development and reworking whose deficiency is characteristic of much psychopathology. Working through in the analytic situation is the reactivation of this normal process, and a good analytic outcome is achieved when the process can continue without the analyst. Properties of the Boltzman algorithm clarify such concepts as "optimal" frustration and anxiety which correspond to working in the area where the stable but not rigid structures emerge in the algorithms operation. These studies are an example of how computer science and artificial intelligence are a potentially rich source for psychoanalytic theory.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference Across Gender Lines

David Raphling M.D. and Judith Chused M.D.

Judith Chused

David L. Raphling, M.D.
Judith F. Chused, M.D.
5225 Connecticut Avenue, N.W.
Washington, DC 20015

Judith Chused  

ABSTRACT

Four clinical examples of oedipal-based transference across gender lines are presented with the aim of illustrating (1) its existence, (2) the defenses against its emergence, and (3) the use of the analyst's gender as both an organizer of and resistance to certain transference manifestations. Factors that contribute to the availability for analysis of cross-gender transference are discussed, as are the resistances and other obstacles to its actualization.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalysis of Time
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalysis of Time

Stephen Kurtz M.S.

Stephen Kurtz

Stephen A. Kurtz, M.S.W.
800 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10025

Stephen Kurtz  

ABSTRACT

The experience of time has been studied psychoanalytically largely through the conceptual frameworks of ego psychology and classical analysis. The implications of self psychology and Lacanian theory for understanding time experience have been relatively little explored. These implications would develop, on the one hand, from Kohut's investigations of fragmentation phenomena and, on the other, from Lacan's concept of the ego and his use of the short session. But time experience cannot be understood entirely in terms of individual histories and the psychoanalytic situation. A preoccupation with time is central to Western culture and is traceable to the social context—thirteenth-century monasticism—in which the clock was first invented.

Time enters the analytic session especially in the handling of beginnings and ends, reflecting divergent notions of the purpose of treatment. The standing of time-frame rules in the different systems defined by ego psychology, self psychology, and Lacanian theory are examined together with their underlying assumptions about the nature of reality. These metaphysical issues—no longer academic but made concretely meaningful in each session—go beyond illuminating the concept of time. Our response to them will determine both the direction of each patient's treatment and the course of psychoanalysis itself.

responseSet.title: Writing about Psychoanalysis: II. Analysts who Write, Patients who Read
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Writing about Psychoanalysis: II. Analysts who Write, Patients who Read

Martin Stein M.D.

Martin Stein

Martin H. Stein, M.D.
8 East 96th Street
New York, NY 10128

Martin Stein  

ABSTRACT

When we make a decision to lecture, or to write for publication, we have begun an activity that will affect every one of our patients. If they attend our lectures, or read our published works, they cannot help being influenced. Even if they do not, they will have some reaction to the focusing of our interest on particular phases of the analysis. This will be true whether or not we intend to present clinical material. These considerations inevitably affect the analyst himself, sometimes inhibiting his writing altogether, sometimes constricting its scope, or worst of all, degrading its quality. The analyst's writing is also almost certain to influence whether and with what success his patient may choose to write.

Lest these considerations suggest that we avoid writing altogether, I emphasize that such a sacrifice is by no means desirable or necessary. Problems of discretion will arise, and may in some cases be virtually insoluble; but there are ways of writing that can overcome most such objections. The analyst's interest in writing should be recognized and its effects, if any, brought into the analysis; so long as the writing itself is done with due regard to the conduct of the analysis and the welfare of the patient, it need have no adverse effects on either.

[[RunningHead]]

Les Années Brunes. Psychoanalysis Under the Third Reich.11

Edited and translated by Jean-Luc Evard. Paris: Confrontations, 1984, 216 pp.

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel
Paris, France

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel  

Remember the days of old, consider the years of many generations: ask thy father, and he will shew thee; thy elders, and they will tell thee.
—Deuteronomy, 32, 7

Where the history of psychoanalysis under Nazi rule in Germany is concerned, it is almost as if one had quite literally obeyed the order: "You are requested to close the eyes" appearing in one of Freud's dreams (1900, p. 317). One must not see. A most tenacious legend, the validity of which, because of its very great likelihood, was not questioned for a long time, holds that analysis was "liquidated" under the Third Reich. Here and there one came across strange accounts dealing with the "Goering Institute" (in the country where I live, that is), but the idea was that this involved a handful of erring psychotherapists gathered around Jung and Schultz-Encke. However, on reading Jones (1961), one finds at least one contradiction. Does he not write that this was the year (we are in 1934) that psychoanalysts fled Germany and the year in which psychoanalysis was "liquidated" from the German Reich, this being one of the rare enterprises Hitler was able to carry out? However, in the same volume, Jones also wonders whether it would not have been better to dissolve the German Society as the Dutch Society had chosen to do when Jewish analysts were forbidden to practice.

In fact, from the mid 70's on, as Régine Lockot points out in a book to be published shortly under the title, Psychoanalysis under Nazism: A

1"The Brown Years." This refers to the brown shirts worn by the Hitler youth, and also to the darkness of those years.1059

responseSet.title: Creativity and Perversion: By Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel.  New York:  Norton, 1984.  172 pp. $22.50.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Creativity and Perversion

By Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel. New York: Norton, 1984. 172 pp. $22.50.

Ethel Person M.D.

Ethel Person

Ethel S. Person, M.D.
New York, N.Y.

Ethel Person  

The study of perversion may be replacing that of narcissism as the hottest new area in which investigation of a clinical entity leads to broad theoretical speculations. Chasseguet-Smirgel's contribution to this burgeoning field explores perversion as an entity in itself, but its more ambitious intent is to place the perverse impulse in a universal dimension. The author alerts us to the ambitiousness of her project when she suggests, in her opening paragraph, that for man "… perversion is one of the essential ways and means he applies in order to push forward the frontiers of what is possible and to unsettle reality" (p. 1).

Her study is brilliant—one might even call it an intellectual tour de force. Yet, paradoxically, while her large vision is stimulating and original, it is in her actual study of perversion that she falls a bit short of the mark, in my opinion retreating from some hard-won insights into perversion. Although her two expositions—that of clinical perversion and of the meaning of perversion within the larger scheme of things—are theoretically intertwined, for purposes of this review, I shall attempt to disentangle them and treat each separately.

While Freud emphasized the perception of the anatomic difference between the sexes as critical to development, Chasseguet-Smirgel sees the essential human tragedy as emanating from a double difference: the anatomic difference between the sexes and the age difference between the generations. Her central thesis is that "the Oedipal tragedy arises largely from the chronological time-lag that exists between the emergence of the boy's desire for his mother and the attainment of his full genital capacity. This time-lag is the result of a kind of a prematurity in the human being… It could even be at the root of the universal taboo of incest, where that which is forbidden is substituted for infantile impotence" (p. 2). While perversion has sometimes been interpreted as the boy's attempt to deny the sexual difference (the fetish as female phallus), Chasseguet-Smirgel broadens this interpretation to suggest that perversion is an attempt to erode the double difference (sex and age) and, therefore, by its nature, an assault on truth and reality.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Structure And Structural Change: An Ego Psychology-object Relations Theory Viewpoint

Otto Kernberg M.D.

Otto Kernberg

Otto F. Kernberg, M.D.
New York Hospital-Cornell Medical Center
21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, NY 10605

Otto Kernberg  

Structural change is usually defined in terms of impulse-defense configurations and modifications in the relations among ego, superego, and id. I am proposing, instead, based on findings regarding the psychopathology of borderline conditions, that impulse and defense should be explored in connection with patterns of internalized object relations, which I see as the "building-blocks" of the tripartite structure. Patterns of object relations, character, and transference developments all reflect the nature of these building-blocks.

responseSet.title: The Concept Of Psychic Structure
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept Of Psychic Structure

Dale Boesky M.D.

Dale Boesky

Dale Boesky, M.D.
755 West Big Beaver Road (Suite 510)
Troy, MI 48084

Dale Boesky  

Originally the term psychic structure was not defined clearly, and subsequently it acquired various different meanings. This paper illustrates that Freud's carryover of his concepts of psychic energy from the libido theory of his topographic model into the tripartite model has been an important source of confusion about the meaning of the term structure. Reification and concretization have also added to misunderstandings of structure. The usual definitions of structure tend to include the functional, organizational, and stability dimensions of structure, but there has been insufficient recognition of the development and functional interrelatedness of the id, ego, and superego. There are important differences in the tripartite model of 1923 and modern structural theory which affect our understanding of the nature of structural change during psychoanalytic treatment. Misunderstandings about the definition of structure have also contributed to perpetuating the spurious dichotomous contrast of pathogenesi due to failures in structural differentiation and pathogenesis due to conflict.

responseSet.title: A Case of Hysteria, with a Note on Biology
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Case of Hysteria, with a Note on Biology

Jon Meyer M.D.

Jon Meyer

Jon K. Meyer, M.D.
2321 East Stratford Court
Milwaukee, WI 53211

Jon Meyer  

ABSTRACT

Psychoanalysis recently has shown interest in the data of neurobiology. This interest has been fostered by the realization that a model of Cartesian mind-body dualism is inadequate to current observation and knowledge. Despite the theoretical appeal of integrating knowledge from the two fields, there have been few attempts at correlation in specific clinical situations. This paper presents a case of hysteria in which the physical manifestations of hyperprolactinemia, galactorrhea, amenorrhea, and infertility are considered in relation to both the brain regulation of prolactin and to unconscious fantasy, motivation, and conflict. Some of the intriguing possibilities and limitations of research at the frontier of psychoanalysis and neurophysiology are discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

The Effect of Therapist Interventions on the Therapeutic Alliance with Borderline Patients

Glen Gabbard M.D. , Leonard Horwitz Ph.D. , Siebolt Frieswyk Ph.D. , Jon Allen Ph.D. , Donald Colson Ph.D. , Gavin Newsom M.S. and Lolafaye Coyne Ph.D.

Lolafaye Coyne

Glen O. Gabbard, M.D.
Leonard Horwitz, Ph.D.
Siebolt Frieswyk, Ph.D.
Jon G. Allen, Ph.D.
Donald B. Colson, Ph.D.
Gavin Newsom, M.S.W.
Lolafaye Coyne, Ph.D.
The Menninger Clinic
Box 829
Topeka, KS 66601

Lolafaye Coyne  

ABSTRACT

The authors draw attention to the problems of establishing and maintaining a therapeutic alliance in the psychotherapy of the borderline patient. They elaborate an extensive methodology designed to study the manner in which shifts in collaboration occur in response to therapist interventions. This report demonstrates how one particular borderline patient increased his ability to collaborate with the therapist in response to a transference focus in the psychotherapy. Methodological problems are noted as are directions for future research. Only a series of patients studied with this or with similar methodology will allow for a sophisticated and empirical rationale for choosing a particular form of psychotherapy for a particular kind of borderline patient.

responseSet.title: Interpretation, Psychoanalysis, and the Philosophy of Mind
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Interpretation, Psychoanalysis, and the Philosophy of Mind

Marcia Cavell Ph.D.

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell, Ph.D.
1570 Olympus Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94708

Marcia Cavell  

ABSTRACT

I suggest that a conflict between two philosophical models of the mind so far unremarked in discussions of psychoanalysis is at the heart of questions about its status as a science, the objectivity of psychoanalytic interpretations, and the nature of the unconscious. In philosophy one model is embodied in the tradition of Descartes, Hobbes, Locke, Kant, among many others, which construes thought as prior to and independent of language. According to this tradition the mind is self-contained and mental contents or "ideas" are essentially subjective phenomena. It follows that knowledge of other minds and the material world is radically problematic. In the second and more contemporary model the phenomenon of meaning is dependent on interactions between minds, and between mind and the world. Since meaning is understood to be intrinsically social, so in an important sense is mind. I develop this second philosophic model, indicating its relevance for psychoanalysis. I also point out some of the contributions of psychoanalysis to philosophy of mind.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transparent Screens

Richard Rosenthal M.D.

Richard Rosenthal

Richard J. Rosenthal, M.D.
435 North Roxbury Drive
Beverly Hills, CA 90210

Richard Rosenthal  

ABSTRACT

There is a kind of transitional phenomenon found among certain borderline patients which is quite distinct from Winnicott's transitional object. These are patients who are preoccupied with maintaining proper physical distance from their objects, in order to regulate anxieties about isolation on the one hand, and identity-annihilating closeness on the other. Since they believe the activity of looking to be intrusive and devouring, hence dangerous, transparent screens are interposed between self and other, and serve as protective barriers. These screens function intrapsychically as well, to split off or hide those aspects of the self felt to be unacceptable.

The analyst may witness the failure of the screen in several ways: it may create too great a distance, isolating the individual and keeping him from life; it may become contaminated by projections and turn into a persecutor, or trap the individual, a state of intolerable claustrophobia; most dramatically, it may suddenly shatter. The latter is associated with psychosis and death, and its appearance may be a harbinger of suicide.

George Silberschatz, Ph.D.
Earl J. Simburg, M.D.
10 Crecienta Lane
Sausalito, CA 94965

Earl Simburg  

ABSTRACT

On the premise that human development is a lifelong process, and with awareness of certain limitations of the stage model, the authors conceptualize a new model based on developmental process. This model is offered as a basic model for the development of mental functions and structures in both childhood and adulthood.
