responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Theory Of Motivational-functional Systems As Psychic Structures

Joseph Lichtenberg M.D.

Joseph Lichtenberg

Joseph D. Lichtenberg, M.D.
1616 Eighteenth Street, N.W.
Washington, DC 20009

Joseph Lichtenberg  

Psychoanalytic theory is not at its core so much a theory of structure as it is a theory of structural motivation. To utilize findings of infant observation and research, psychoanalysts must be open to new conceptions of psychic organization. I propose five motivational-functional systems: the need to fulfill physiological requirements, the need for attachment and affiliation, the need for assertion and exploration, the need to react aversively through antagonism and/or withdrawal, the need for sensual and sexual pleasure. Each is built around a fundamental need; each is based on behavior observable in the neonatal period; each may be the dominant motive expressed by an analysand's wishes during an analytic hour. For each motivational-functional system, affects are central.

For the purpose of this evaluation The Foundations of Psychoanalysis can best be divided into two parts: Grünbaum's critique of the hermeneutic understanding of psychoanalysis, and his critique of the scientific status of psychoanalysis. At first glance, this two-fold nature of Grünbaum's argument may appear paradoxical. Grünbaum first attacks the hermeneutic approach to psychoanalytic theory (Ricoeur, Habermas, et al.) for its failure to appreciate the scientific nature of Freud's hypotheses. [[RunningHead]]

Superego Aspects of Entitlement (In Rigid Characters)

Stanley Coen M.D.

Stanley Coen

Stanley J. Coen, M.D.
305 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10023

Stanley Coen  

ABSTRACT

Dynamic conflict among feelings of entitlement, defectiveness, and deprivation is discussed from the perspective of superego defense against dangerously aggressive (and sexual) wishes. These negative feelings and negative self-images are exploited so as to appease the superego in the face of one's hostile aggression: that one is justified, that there are extenuating circumstances for one's hatred and destructiveness. The "oral" clamor of deprivation and entitlement, together with dependency, submissiveness, and defensive uncertainty, serve a screening function for hostile aggressive wishes, from any developmental level. Typically, attitudes of entitlement are contradictory, unclear, and wavering, because of conflict between differing ego ideal images, and conflict between superego, and ego and id, about the sadistic extractiveness in these attitudes. Attitudes of entitlement are contributed to by past trauma, deprivation, abuse, teasing overtimulation coupled with neglect, or alternating indulgence and deprivation, as well as identification with certain parental attitudes—exploitation and extractiveness—and denial of the need for the superego to asses reality accurately.

responseSet.title: Kohut's Method of Interpretation: A Critique
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Kohut's Method of Interpretation: A Critique

Philip Rubovits-Seitz M.D.

Philip Rubovits-Seitz

Philip Rubovits-Seitz, M.D.
2623 Woodley Place, N.W.
Washington, DC 20008

Philip Rubovits-Seitz  

ABSTRACT

To cope with the obscure, complexly overdetermined, and unstable nature of unconscious meanings, Freud developed a pluralistic methodology that employs a wide variety of interpretive strategies and procedures. Conversely, Kohut proposed a radically abbreviated interpretive approach based on the single, subjective method of empathy. This report reevaluates Kohut's monistic interpretive methodology: (1) The principal features of Kohut's interpretive method are reviewed and evaluated. (2) Case material and interpretations from Kohut's final book are used to compare his unidimensional approach with the pluralistic methodology of traditional interpretation. (3) The epistemologic liabilities of Kohut's interpretive method are delineated and discussed. (4) Methodologically more appropriate strategies for improving clinical interpretation are presented.

responseSet.title: Freud's Three Theories of Love in the Light of Later Developments
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Three Theories of Love in the Light of Later Developments

Martin Bergmann Ph.D.

Martin Bergmann

Martin S. Bergmann, Ph.D.
1136 Fifth Avenue
New York, NY 10128

Martin Bergmann  

ABSTRACT

Evidence is presented that Freud developed three very different theories on love. These theories were not integrated into a coherent theory. In subsequent developments each theory had its own history. The paper discusses the history of the controversy on the genital character, the relation between love and gender identity, between love and narcissism, the hierarchical structure of the capacity to love, and the relation between love and object loss. The impact of some concepts such as symbiosis and the rapprochement subphase on the understanding of conflicts in loving is raised. While at present differences in emphasis make it difficult to build a coherent psychoanalytic theory of love, it is productive to bring divergent views in touch with each other. A unified theory of love based on psychoanalytic observations is suggested.

responseSet.title: Assessment Of Structural Change In Psychoanalytic Therapy And Research
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Assessment Of Structural Change In Psychoanalytic Therapy And Research

Robert Wallerstein M.D.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.
Langley Porter Institute 401 Parnassus Avenue
Box 40C
San Francisco, CA 94143

Robert Wallerstein  

The concepts of "psychic structure" and of "structural change" are among the most central but also among the most problematic within the psychoanalytic theory of therapy and of change. The unclarities in the range of different definitions, meanings, and usages that exist in the literature are reviewed. Among them is that "structure" and "structural change" are understood differently depending on one's theoretical point of view in psychoanalysis. Clinical material is presented from a research-studied completed psychoanalysis that illustrates these various theoretical dilemmas and unclarities as they emerge in the effort to assess empirically the degree and kind of structural change that occurred in this treatment. A new empirical research approach to this effort to delineate "structure" and "structural change" is then presented. This is done by formulating assessable "psychological capacities" (character propensities) that adherents of all prevailing psychoanalytic theoretical perspectives can agree ar attributes that comprehensively describe personality functioning and that will necessarily shift if there is "underlying" change in intrapsychic structures, however those intrapsychic structures or that structural change are conceptualized. A table is presented which lists fifteen thus far designated "psychological capacities" and indicates the two (or more) directions of pathologic deviation on each. A research program based on these conceptions is outlined.

responseSet.title: Patient's Sleep on the Analytic Couch
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Patient's Sleep on the Analytic Couch

Lawrence Inderbitzin M.D.

Lawrence Inderbitzin

Lawrence B. Inderbitzin, M.D.
Emory University Psychoanalytic Center
1711 Uppergate Drive, N.E.
Atlanta, GA 30322

Lawrence Inderbitzin  

ABSTRACT

Published reports about sleep on the couch have primarily emphasized its preoedipal (especially oral) determinants and defensive purposes. A detailed case is presented in which sleep became the central symptom of the transference neurosis. The primary determinants of the symptom were from the phallic-oedipal stage of development. Like other symptoms, the sleep symptom was a reliving of earlier experiences and was analyzed primarily through the transference. The analytic data from the case are used to illustrate resistance as an ally in the psychoanalytic process.

responseSet.title: Day Residue and Screen Memory in Freud's Dream of the Botanical Monograph
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Day Residue and Screen Memory in Freud's Dream of the Botanical Monograph

Stanley Palombo M.D.

Stanley Palombo

Stanley R. Palombo, M.D.
5225 Connecticut Avenue, N. W.
Washington, DC 20015

Stanley Palombo  

ABSTRACT

Freud's theory of dream construction allowed the censorship to intervene only when a repressed infantile wish emerged from the unconscious. In his (1899) paper on screen memories, however, he proposed a mechanism for the defensive displacement of current events as they are sorted for introduction into permanent memory. I suggest that Freud was actually describing the conflictual process through which the day residue of the dream is formed. Day residue and screen memory are closely related as elements of the dreamer's present and past experience displaced from his more central instinctual concerns.

Freud's dream of the botanical monograph clearly illustrates this relation. Substituted day residues were matched in the dream with relatively innocuous memories of past events of similar cognitive and affective significance. By retracing the substitutions, one can see how a current conflict over Fliess's role in the writing of the dream book recapitulated a series of Freud's earlier conflicts concerning his father and the power of books.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Interpersonal World of the Infant: A View from Psychoanalysis and Developmental Psychology

By Daniel N. Stern. New York: Basic Books, 1985, 304 pp., $23.95.

Robert Emde M.D.

Robert Emde

Robert N. Emde, M.D.
Denver, CO

Robert Emde  

This is an important work which will be widely read in both psychoanalysis and developmental psychology. Stern's perspective is based on recent research which indicates a strong biological preparedness for social interaction in the human infant. Biological preparedness, however, means that it is all the more crucial for us to focus on the infant's inner experience to understand development, and the problems of tracing continuities in the midst of change. The scientific argument for a psychology of experience is bold enough during a time of life when the child cannot talk to us, but Stern goes further. He stretches our minds to consider not only the subjective world of the infant, but also the intersubjective world involving infant and caregiver. The book's title is well chosen.

228

responseSet.title: Compulsive Eating: Obesity and Related Phenomena
responseSet.authorMast: Pietro Castelnuovo-Tedesco and  Lynn Whisnant Reiser
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Compulsive Eating: Obesity and Related Phenomena

Pietro Castelnuovo-Tedesco M.D. and Lynn Reiser M.D.

Lynn Reiser

Lynn Reiser  

PIETRO CASTELNUOVO-TEDESCO INTRODUCED THE panel by pointing out the relation of obesity to anorexia, bulimia, and compulsive eating. He stressed that all these conditions may be seen as facets of a broad category of eating disorders characterized by disturbance of the body image, concern over being fat, and the perception of being too fat. These entities also are related to other forms of psychopathology, such as depression, addictions, and gambling. He noted that it is one of the contributions of psychoanalysis that all these conditions can be seen as related to one another clinically, developmentally, and dynamically.

Castelnuovo-Tedesco reviewed the early psychoanalytic contributions to the understanding of obesity, which emphasized the vicissitudes of the oral drive and its impact on character formation. From a more current perspective, eating disorders are noteworthy not only because of the frequency with which they occur, but also because of the close relation between drive discharge and the problem of maintaining adequate controls. Although the principal symptom expresses a disturbance in drive discharge, incapacity of the ego to perform its necessary functions and significant pathology in self-representation are also impressive features.

Personality characteristics frequently found in obese people are, first, that they often use food for self-soothing, substitutive gratification and as a replacement for disappointing objects; in this way they achieve a kind of independence and autonomy (not consistent with the stereotype of the orally dependent personality) ordinarily derived from the contribution of the later developmental stages (primarily, anal and phallic-oedipal). A second important characteristic, shared with those who have other impulse disorders, is that they do not readily seek treatment, and when they do, it is often one that promises

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 21, 1985.

Panelists: Lawrence Deutsch, Ira L. Mintz, Christopher V. Rowland, Jr., C. Philip Wilson; Discussant: Stephen L. Post.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Assault on Truth: Freud's Suppression of the Seduction Theory

By Jeffrey M. Masson. New York: Farrar, Strauss & Giroux, 1984, 336 pp., $16.95.

Lawrence Freedman M.D.

Lawrence Freedman

Lawrence Z. Freedman, M.D.
Chicago, IL

Lawrence Freedman  

Hysteria, the most ancient of medical diseases, has become one of the most controversial in psychoanalytical discourse. In The Assault on Truth: Freud's Suppression of the Seduction Theory, Masson challenges Freud's rejection of his first theory of the origin of hysteria from infantile sexual seduction: Freud suppressed his seduction theory because he feared opprobrium and he craved acceptance. Masson assails Freud for stigmatizing his patients as liars. Consequently, everything that followed the recantation has rendered psychoanalysis theoretically sterile and clinically ineffective.

Masson uses the word "truth" as synonymous with the recalled reality of childhood sexual seduction as described by Freud in 1896. The general theory of infantile sexuality, which was later described, is seen by Masson as an assault on truth and a suppression of the seduction theory. The argument is ad hominem; the polemic, presented in this mode, is itself a fallacy.

183

[[RunningHead]]

The Practice of Supportive Psychotherapy

By David S. Werman. New York: Brunner/Mazel, 1984, 192 pp. $25.00

Leonard Horwitz Ph.D.

Leonard Horwitz

Leonard Horwitz, Ph.D.
Topeka, KS

Leonard Horwitz  

This is probably the first full-length book on supportive psychotherapy written by a psychoanalyst, indeed, by a training analyst. Most mental health practitioners regard supportive psychotherapy as a treatment requiring a lesser degree of training and skill and hence most appropriate for the novice or least well-trained. Werman contests this point of view, contending that supportive psychotherapy is a modality in its own right: "Supportive psychotherapy is not a vague alternative to insight-oriented treatment; it is a well-defined form of psychotherapy and represents a logical application of psychoanalytic concepts to a particular class of patients whose characteristics can usually be clearly identified" (p. ix). He believes that supportive treatment is highly demanding on the therapist inasmuch as he or she is usually dealing with patients who are more disturbed and whose defenses are more primitive than is true of patients in insight-oriented therapies, so that the repertoire of therapeutic interventions is necessarily broader and more varied. He recommends, therefore, that beginners should undertake such treatment only slowly and with sufficient supervision.

The author makes clear from the start that he is not presenting a scholarly review of the subject. He does not include a survey of the literature on the subject. This slim book is only intended as a distillate of his clinical experiences, both as therapist and supervisor. Like the goals associated with the modality of therapy he is describing, the author's aims appear to be limited insofar as he is not attempting in any systematic way to build upon an existing theory of supportive psychotherapy, sparse as it is. Rather, the book has a practical, applied orientation, while systematic principles tend to be deemphasized.

Patients for whom supportive treatment is indicated are (1) those who show significant ego defects of a chronic nature or (2)

responseSet.documentRefXML: Esman, A. H. (1988) Art and Psyche: By Ellen Handler Spitz. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1985, 188 pp. $18.95.. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 36:199-203

responseSet.title: Art and Psyche: By Ellen Handler Spitz.  New Haven:  Yale Univ. Press, 1985, 188 pp. $18.95.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Art and Psyche

By Ellen Handler Spitz. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1985, 188 pp. $18.95.

Aaron Esman M.D.

Aaron Esman

Aaron H. Esman, M.D.
New York, NY

Aaron Esman  

The interface between art and psychoanalysis has in recent years become increasingly an object of systematic scrutiny. This methodologic scrutiny is bound to benefit both fields of scholarship, to the extent that it sharpens conceptual thinking and enhances the capacity

199

of scholars to assess their respective roles in the interdisciplinary study. Thus we have had such recent volumes as F. L. Kuhn's contribution to the psychoanalytic theory of art, P. J. Mahony's study of Freud's literary style, M. Skura's critical review of the applications of psychoanalysis to literature and, in this new work, a review of some of the fundamental questions in the area of so-called "applied" analysis as it relates to the arts in general and the plastic arts in particular.

Dr. Spitz has sought here to define systematically the various ways in which both analysts and estheticians have attempted to use psychoanalytic ideas to enlarge our grasp of the artistic enterprise. She distinguishes three basic approaches—each more or less associated with an aspect of evolving psychoanalytic theory. Freud's Leonardo essay serves appropriately as the prototype of the pathographic method, in which the emphasis has been on demonstration of the connections between the artist's work and the unconscious conflicts that, presumably, determined it. In this mode the work of art is treated as equivalent to the symptom or the dream, and biographical data are used in a manner analogous to the analysand's associations, with the author interpretively connecting unconscious configurations.

The second approach Spitz identifies is that in which the work of art itself becomes the focus of study. Here the artifact is treated as autonomous, and the interpreter seeks to explicate the role of defensive and integrative factors, largely through the study of the nuances of form and style. This mode of approach is congruent with, and largely an expression of, ego psychology, as well as being consistent (with regard to literature, at any rate) with the "New Criticism," exemplified here particularly by the work of William Empson.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Religious Experience

By William W. Meissner. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1984, 254 pp., $26.00.

Mortimer Ostow M.D.

Mortimer Ostow

Mortimer Ostow, M.D.
Riverdale, NY

Mortimer Ostow  

206

Members of contiguous groups in society, during intervals of comfort and confidence, reach out to each other in interest and friendship, but at times of crisis withdraw again. Psychoanalysis and religion share a concern with cosmology, fraternity, community, salvation, and transcendence, each defined in its own terms. Yet the early psychoanalytic community, which can be viewed as a secular sect within Judaism (Ostow, 1982), set itself in opposition to the Jewish religion as well as to all others. And Freud's disparaging views of religion induced recirprocal disparagement of psychoanalysis by the religious. Mutual antagonism between related populations with common interests have occurred frequently in history, as Freud (1921) observed.

This situation of divisiveness between similar and related disciplines, and between the adherents of these disciplines who nevertheless resemble each other in many ways, invites boundary crossing. While in the early years of psychoanalytic history, few crossed this boundary—the best known was Oskar Pfister—recently there have been a number of religious individuals like Meissner who have made a full commitment to psychoanalysis. Currently an increasing number of members of both high and low cultural groups in our society have been approaching religion with interest and enthusiasm, many psychoanalysts among them. Presumably it is this situation that has prompted Dr. Meissner to write this book.

His first task was to deal with Freud's well-known hostility to religion. Meissner attributes this hostility to Freud's oedipal ambivalence and promises to treat Freud's personal views as irrelevant to his argument, a promise not completely fulfilled. The author claims that Freud's account of the origin of religious expression is too narrow, and that modern findings concerning narcissism and object relations permit us to appreciate "the integrative and self-sustaining aspects of religious phenomena."

He reviews Moses and Monotheism and observes that Freud's arguments cannot be corroborated by recently discovered archeologic evidence and texts. Presumably, if Freud is not to be trusted in this argument, the whole of his attitude toward religion can be questioned.

León Grinberg

León Grinberg, M.D.
Madrid, Spain

León Grinberg  

It is not an easy task to write a brief but sufficiently clear critical review of this important contribution by the late David Liberman. This work is the second corrected and enlarged edition of an earlier work written in 1976. The difficulty of this review lies not only in the breadth of the two volumes, but also in the density and complexity of the concepts developed in them.

responseSet.title: Concurrent Individual and Individual-In-A-Group Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Concurrent Individual and Individual-In-A-Group Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy

Joseph Schachter M.D.

Joseph Schachter

Joseph Schachter, M.D.
5400 Darlington Road
Pittsburgh, PA 15217

Joseph Schachter  

ABSTRACT

This paper's thesis is that concurrent individual-in-a-group and individual psychoanalytic psychotherapy can be conducted in conformance with psychoanalytic principles of treatment as well as can individual psychoanalytic psychotherapy alone. American psychoanalysts have shown little interest in group psychotherapy, probably because of earlier criticism that transference is diluted by the greater reality of the therapist in group psychotherapy. This is a misconception extrapolated from the mirror model of dyadic analytic technique. The criticism was formulated during a period when that model was prominent and there was little awareness that the actual personal relationship between patient and analyst played an important facilitating role in the dyadic analytic process, including providing a basis for investiture of transference. Also, the criticism was based on one-session-per-week group psychotherapy, whereas concurrent individual-in-a-group and individual psychoanalytic psychotherapy utilizes two group sessions plus one or two individual sessions per week, enabling a more intensive patient-therapist relationship. Concurrent group and individual psychoanalytic psychotherapy constitutes a contribution to the widening scope of application of psychoanalytic treatment.

responseSet.title: On Wandering Terminology
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Wandering Terminology

Mark Blechner Ph.D.

Mark Blechner

Mark J. Blechner, Ph.D.
145 Central Park West
New York, NY 10023

Mark Blechner  

October 14, 1986

A tradition of wandering terminology is evident in psychoanalysis and is a cause for serious concern. The typical pattern is as follows: first, a term is coined; then it is used by another author in a slightly expanded or different way. This process is repeated until vastly different usages become current, some of which may contradict others. Ultimately, such a trend eliminates the time-saving value of a referential vocabulary, a human capacity that has been evolving over millennia.

I am not sure that this recklessness with denotative meanings will halt the course of psychoanalytic evolution. At the very least, it causes much needless confusion. Consider how cumbersome the word "empathy" has already become, with the need to expostulate on its myriad meanings (e.g., Reed, 1984.)

"Countertransference" is the focus of the present communication. Freud's original use of the word (1910; in German, it is Gegenuebertragung) has been expanded many times. Authors have used it to refer to the general transference to the patient by the analyst, based, in part, on his own history; the analyst's responses, neurotic or not, to the patient's transference; and ultimately to all of the analyst's reactions to the patient. However different and confusing these varied usages are, one convention holds—countertransference always refers to the analyst.

Most recently, however, another meaning has been added which I am afraid will lead to more confusion than clarification. This is Loewald's (1986) use of the word "countertransference" to refer to the patient's responses to the analyst's transference. Loewald (1986) writes: "The patient's behavior, while importantly determined by transference displacements from his past, often is triggered by the analyst's behavior or words, so that it represents also countertransference on the part of the patient" (p. 279).

555

responseSet.title: On Learning from the Patient: By Patrick Casement.  London:  Tavistock Publ., 1985, 232 pp., $28.00; paperback, $13.95.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Learning from the Patient

By Patrick Casement. London: Tavistock Publ., 1985, 232 pp., $28.00; paperback, $13.95.

Richard Fox M.D.

Richard Fox

Richard Fox, M.D.
Santa Ana, California

Richard Fox  

Our standard model psychoanalytic technique is based on Freud's early psychoanalytic work, including his self-analysis. The consequences of these origins are reflected in the one-sided nature of our model technique, which is based on a one-person psychology. Its limits are strained when we attempt to formulate into theoretical terms something which has long been recognized by the analyst at

785

work—namely that the analytic experience goes beyond the dimension of cognitive insight implied by our model technique.

Patrick Casement, a British analyst, has written a book on psychoanalytic technique which is addressed to the interactional aspects of the therapeutic process. In making his contribution to our understanding of the psychoanalytic process, Casement suggests that the nature of the patient's experience of the therapeutic relationship is at least as important a therapeutic factor as any gain in cognitive insight. To highlight the analyst's participation in the process, Casement suggests we consider: the nature of the analytic presence; the mutual influence of patient on analyst and analyst on patient; and the subjective response of each of the participants, how it is processed and how it is interpreted.

Casement's view of the analytic presence is rooted in Winnicott's notions about the child analysand's finding of and using the object. The model proposed is based on an experiential reenactment rather than an instinctual displacement. It is the responsibility of the analyst to be unobtrusively present for the patient in such a way that he lends himself to be used by the patient in the service of this attempt at reliving. To preserve the creative potential of this space, the analyst must learn how to remain close enough to what the patient is experiencing for this to have a feeling impact on him (the analyst) while preserving sufficient distance to continue to function as analyst.

Within this highly subjective, interactive space, this sphere of mutual influence, Casement feels that every analyst communicates far more about himself than is usually recognized, and that this contributes to the patient's experience of the transference. As he demonstrates with a series of candid clinical presentations, therapists inevitably make mistakes in what Balint has called this "harmonious mix-up." The patient then may use these errors in unconscious ways not only as a vehicle for the resistance, but as a way to further the analytic process. The ensuing work on these episodes enables the analyst to learn from the patient and to further the analytic work.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Immortal Atatürk. A Psychobiography

By Vamik D. Volkan and Norman Iskowitz. Chicago: Univ. Chicago Press, 1984, xxv + 374 pp., $37.50.

L. Boyer

L. Boyer

L. Bryce Boyer
Berkeley, California

L. Boyer  

The Immortal Atatürk is the felicitous product of cross-disciplinary collaboration between eminent scholars, the psychoanalyst Volkan and the historian Iskowitz. Consistently interesting throughout, it is the result of seven years of extensive and intensive research into Turkish history and the effects of social structure—especially political—and socialization on the development of Mustafa Kemal Atatürk and, in turn, his effect on Turkish customs and both intramural and international politics. The charismatic Atatürk became the "founder of the modern Republic of Turkey and one of the architects of the twentieth century" (p. 12). The research was enhanced by Volkan's fluency in Turkish, one result of his having been a Cypriot Turk. The Immortal Atatürk convincingly affirms Mack's (1978) opinion that psychoanalysis enables the biographer to "understand character, to provide psychological coherence to the pattern of life, to fathom motivation, and to understand how intrapsychic conflicts are transformed and surmounted in the subject's life" (p. 108).

803

responseSet.title: Fear of Being Fat: The Treatment of Anorexia Nervosa and Bulimia (2nd Ed.).: Edited by C. Philip Wilson with the assistance of Charles C. Hogan and Ira L. Mintz. North Vale, N.J.:  Aronson, 1985.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fear of Being Fat: The Treatment of Anorexia Nervosa and Bulimia (2nd Ed.).

Edited by C. Philip Wilson with the assistance of Charles C. Hogan and Ira L. Mintz. North Vale, N.J.: Aronson, 1985.

Paul Garfinkel M.D.

Paul Garfinkel

Paul E. Garfinkel, M.D.
Toronto, Canada

Paul Garfinkel  

The contributors to this book are members of the Psychosomatic Study Group of the Psychoanalytic Association of New York, and specialize in the treatment of psychosomatic disorders. This group was led initially by Melitta Sperling and, since 1973, by Wilson. The purpose of this book is to provide a framework for understanding both the bulimic and restricting forms of anorexia nervosa and, based on this,

839

an approach to treatment. The treatment emphasizes a focus on pregenital conflicts, part-object relations, and the central issue of the transference. Psychoanalysis is described repeatedly as the treatment of choice for these patients, even when they are extremely emaciated.

The 18 chapters have been organized into five sections. The first, on predisposing factors, consists of two chapters by Wilson. This is followed by four chapters on psychodynamic structure; these begin with an older paper of Sperling's, giving her views of the syndrome. Section Three deals with transference and countertransference; Section Four, with treatment; and Section Five, with special issues such as the amenorrhea in anorexia nervosa and problems with the use of medication in bulimia.

There are strengths to the volume. These include (1) the emphasis by various contributors on the problems of control experienced by these patients; (2) the careful distinction between bulimic and restricting forms of the illness and the characterization of the bulimic as having an impulse disorder; (3) a large component to each chapter consists of clinical case descriptions; and (4) the material on transference and countertransference is particularly helpful. However, there are serious deficiencies. The reader is left with an impression that the contributors are either unaware of other work on similar subjects or deliberately ignore the writings of others. For example, Wilson describes the central feature of the disorder as a "fat phobia," stating that this term should replace the misnomer "anorexia" nervosa. This concept is even described as a "major new hypothesis," without ever acknowledging that others have written on this for over 20 years.

Patrick Mahony

Patrick J. Mahony, Ph.D.
Outremont, Quebec
Canada

Patrick Mahony  

I shall not be superficial if I indicate right away that on the cover of this book is a reproduction of Brueghel's Tower of Babel, which shows an architectural structure that stands not only incomplete, but also skewed. The reproduction is graphically appropriate for the adventurousness of the collected volume whose unwieldiness is foreshadowed in its translated title: Languages: the Second Psychoanalytic Meetings of Aix-en-Provence (1983)—Language in Psychoanalysis. But the unwieldy title is also deceptive.

[[RunningHead]]

Dynamic Therapy of the Older Patient

By Wayne A. Myers. New York: Aronson, 1984, 257 pp., $25.00.

Pearl King

Pearl King

Pearl King
London, U.K.

Pearl King  

This book deals with an area of psychotherapeutic work that is gradually gaining more attention from the medical and psychological professions. After far too many years when the topic of the possibility of psychoanalyzing older patients, that is, patients over 40 or 50 years, was looked upon with horror or at the least, with disfavor, the climate of opinion seems gradually to be changing.

In 1979, at the first Congress of the International Psychoanalytic Association to be held in the United States, I presented a paper which dealt with the psychoanalysis of patients in the second half of life. During the discussion of this paper I emphasized that such people were a neglected, although a rapidly growing group of potential patients, and that it was not fair to exclude them from the possibility of obtaining psychoanalytic help, purely on the criterion of age.

1087
